Dried, unsalted fish, commonly known as 'turrur fiskur', prepared by dehydration without salt addition. Typically consumed as a preserved food, this product retains natural flavor and is often used in traditional or regional cuisines. May include various fish species, but not salted or smoked.

Dried and salted fish, referred to as 'klippfiskur', prepared by combining dehydration with salt for preservation. Commonly used in traditional diets, especially in coastal regions. May include species like herring or mackerel, with salt content enhancing flavor and shelf life.

Freshwater and marine fish species such as tilapia, pangasius, catfish, carp, eel, and snakehead, prepared as dried fish. These include a wide range of species from tropical and temperate waters, often used in Asian and African cuisines. Typically dried and sometimes smoked for preservation.

Polar cod (Boreogadus saida), dried and prepared as a preserved food. Found in Arctic waters, this species is used for its high oil content and mild flavor. Commonly consumed in northern regions and processed for long-term storage.

Dried fish of unspecified species, not included in other categories. May include small pelagic fish, bottom-dwellers, or regional varieties. Typically preserved by dehydration, often with salt or without, for use in traditional or local diets.

Herrings (Clupea harengus, Clupea pallasii), dried and preserved. Commonly found in North Atlantic and North Pacific waters, these small oily fish are processed for long-term storage and used in salads, sauces, or as snacks. Often consumed in salted or unsalted forms.

Anchovies (Engraulis spp.), dried and preserved. Small, silvery fish found in tropical and temperate waters, commonly used in Mediterranean and Asian cuisines. Dried anchovies are often used as flavor enhancers or in sauces and soups.

Atlantic halibut (Hippoglossus hippoglossus), salted or preserved. Large flatfish found in the North Atlantic, prized for its firm, white flesh. Typically processed for export or local consumption, often salted or dried for preservation.

Dried fish of unspecified species, not covered by other codes. May include small or medium-sized fish, often preserved by dehydration. Typically used in traditional or regional cuisines, without salt or with salt, depending on preparation.

Salted herrings (Clupea harengus, Clupea pallasii). These small, oily fish are preserved by salting, commonly found in North Atlantic and North Pacific waters. Used in traditional dishes, sauces, or as snacks, often with a distinct briny flavor.

Salted cod (Gadus morhua, Gadus ogac, Gadus macrocephalus), flaked or whole. Found in Atlantic waters, this species is salted for preservation, often used in traditional dishes like fish cakes or soups. The salted preparation enhances flavor and extends shelf life.

Anchovies (Engraulis spp.), salted. Small, silvery fish found in tropical and temperate waters, preserved by salting. Commonly used in Mediterranean and Asian cuisines, often in sauces, soups, or as snacks for their intense umami flavor.

They have a firm, slightly sweet flavor and are popular in Asian, Mediterranean, and European seafood dishes.

Live, fresh, or chilled squid species including Ommastrephes spp. (excluding Ommastrephes sagittatus), Nototodarus spp., and Sepioteuthis spp. These are large, fast-swimming cephalopods harvested from open ocean waters. They are typically prepared by frying or boiling and have a firm, mild flavor, commonly used in Asian and Mediterranean cuisines.

Live, fresh, or chilled European flying squid (Todarodes sagittatus), also known as Ommastrephes sagittatus. This marine cephalopod is found in the North Atlantic and Mediterranean. It has a firm, tender texture and is commonly prepared by frying or boiling. Used in Japanese, Mediterranean, and European seafood dishes.

White wheat seed, intended for sowing, typically used in agriculture for cultivating grain crops, often processed into flour or used as animal feed, and characterized by its pale color and high starch content.
Other white wheat products, including by-products or non-seed forms such as processed grains, damaged kernels, or wheat used for non-sowing purposes like animal feed or industrial applications.
Spelt, a type of ancient wheat with a hard, dense kernel, used primarily for baking and milling, often preferred for its nutty flavor and gluten-free properties, grown in temperate regions.
Common wheat and meslin (a blend of wheat and rye), typically used for flour production, with meslin providing a unique texture and flavor profile, often used in breads and pastries.
Other wheat products not classified elsewhere, including damaged grains, waste, or by-products such as bran, chaff, or gluten-containing residues from milling or processing.
Other wheat products, including non-seed forms such as processed grains, flour, or waste materials from milling, not fitting into specific categories like spelt or meslin.
Rye seed, intended for sowing, typically used in agriculture to cultivate cereal crops, often used in bread-making due to its robust flavor and high fiber content.
Other rye products, including processed grains, by-products, or non-seed forms such as rye flour, bran, or waste materials from milling or industrial use.
Barley seed, intended for sowing, used primarily in agriculture for grain production, often processed into malt for brewing or animal feed due to its high energy content.
Other barley products, including processed grains, by-products, or non-seed forms such as malt, flour, or waste materials from milling or brewing.
Oats seed, intended for sowing, used in agriculture for cereal crops, often processed into oatmeal, animal feed, or used in baking due to its high fiber and protein content.
Other oats products, including processed grains, by-products, or non-seed forms such as oat flour, bran, or waste materials from milling or food processing.
Corn seed of three-cross hybrid varieties, genetically engineered for high yield and uniform growth, typically used in commercial agriculture for food, feed, or industrial purposes.
Corn seed of simple hybrid varieties, bred for specific traits like drought resistance or high yield, used in agriculture for food, feed, or industrial applications.
Other corn products, including processed grains, by-products, or non-seed forms such as cornmeal, syrup, or waste materials from milling or processing.
Other corn products not classified elsewhere, including non-seed forms such as corn oil, gluten, or industrial by-products from processing.
Other corn products, including non-seed forms such as corn flour, bran, or waste materials from milling or food processing, not fitting into specific categories.
Oryza sativa seed, intended for sowing, typically used in rice cultivation, with varieties adapted to different climates and soil conditions, often processed into staple food or animal feed.
Round-grain rice, characterized by spherical kernels, often used in Asian cuisine, processed into white or brown rice, and suitable for boiling or steaming.
Medium-grain rice, with kernels that are slightly elongated but not overly long, often used in dishes requiring a moist texture, such as risotto or sushi.
Long-grain rice with a length/width ratio between 2 and 3, often used in dishes requiring fluffy grains, such as pilafs or stir-fries, common in Latin American and Southeast Asian cuisines.
Long-grain rice with a length/width ratio of 3 or more, often used in dishes requiring distinct grains, such as paella or Indian curries, prized for its fluffiness and aroma.

Rice grains with a length-to-width ratio greater than 2 but less than 3, typically elongated and medium-sized, used for culinary purposes such as cooking or processing into rice products.
Rice grains with a length-to-width ratio of 3 or more, usually long and slender, often used in Asian cuisines for dishes requiring long-grain rice, such as sushi or pilafs.
Rice grains that are round in shape, typically small and uniform, often used for making congee, risotto, or other dishes requiring a soft, rounded texture.
Rice grains with a medium grain size, neither round nor long, often used in everyday cooking for dishes like fried rice or rice salads, offering a balanced texture.
Rice grains with a length-to-width ratio greater than 2 but less than 3, similar to code 10063065, often elongated and suitable for general culinary use.
Rice grains with a length-to-width ratio of 3 or more, long and slender, commonly used in Asian and global cuisines for dishes requiring long-grain rice.
Broken rice, consisting of rice grains that are fractured or split during milling, often used for animal feed, rice-based porridge, or as a cost-effective ingredient in cooking.
Hybrid sorghum seeds, specifically bred for high yield and disease resistance, used primarily for agricultural planting in arid or semi-arid regions.
Other sorghum, including non-hybrid varieties or unspecified types, used for food, animal feed, or industrial applications such as biofuel or starch production.
Other sorghum not specified elsewhere, typically used for animal feed, human consumption, or processing into sorghum-based products like syrup or flour.
Buckwheat, a pseudocereal grown for its seeds, used in traditional dishes like soba noodles, pancakes, or as a gluten-free grain substitute.
Horsegram or millet seeds intended for planting, used to cultivate crops for human or animal consumption, often in drought-resistant agriculture.
Other types of millet or horsegram, including non-specified varieties, used for food, animal feed, or local culinary traditions in regions like India or Africa.
Canary seed, small and hard, primarily used as bird feed, but also occasionally in human diets or as a source of oil and protein.
Fonio, a small-seeded cereal from the Digitaria genus, cultivated in West Africa for its high nutritional value and used in porridges or as a flour substitute.
Quinoa, a nutrient-rich seed from the Chenopodium quinoa plant, used as a grain substitute in meals, soups, or as a protein source in vegetarian diets.
Triticale, a hybrid cereal resulting from crossing wheat and rye, used for animal feed, human consumption, or as a high-yield crop in temperate regions.
Other cereals not specified in preceding codes, including minor or regional varieties, used for food, feed, or industrial purposes.

Flour made from hard wheat, specifically durum wheat, which is high in protein and commonly used in pasta and semolina products. The flour is typically fine and powdery, with a pale yellow color, and retains the structural integrity needed for durum-based foods.

Flour derived from a blend of common wheat and spelt, both cereal grains. This flour combines the characteristics of soft wheat with the nutty flavor and higher fiber content of spelt, suitable for baking breads, pastas, and other baked goods requiring a balanced texture and nutritional profile.

Flour made from a mixture of wheat and rye, commonly called meslin flour. This blend offers a unique flavor and texture, combining the mildness of wheat with the robustness of rye. It is used in artisanal breads, pastries, and regional cuisines where mixed flours are traditional.

Corn flour with a fat content not exceeding 1.5% by weight. This low-fat variant is typically used in food processing and baking where fat content must be strictly controlled. It is fine in texture and commonly used in corn-based products such as tortillas, cakes, and industrial starches.

Corn flour not falling under other specific categories. This includes all corn flours not classified by fat content or other criteria, such as those used in thickening agents, animal feed, or industrial applications where no specific standard applies.

Flour made from barley, a cereal grain used in baking and brewing. Barley flour is coarse and slightly sweet, often used in traditional breads, soups, and as a gluten-free alternative in some applications. It is typically pale beige or light brown in color.

Flour made from oats, a cereal grain rich in soluble fiber. Oat flour is light and creamy in texture, often used in baking, smoothies, and as a gluten-free alternative. It has a mild, slightly sweet flavor and is commonly used in oatmeal-based products.

Flour made from rice, a staple grain in many cultures. Rice flour is fine, white, and gluten-free, used in cakes, noodles, and gluten-free baking. It is commonly processed from long-grain or short-grain rice and has a neutral flavor and smooth texture.

Flour made from rye, a cereal grain with a dark, robust flavor. Rye flour is coarser than wheat flour and is used in dense breads, traditional European breads, and as a gluten-free alternative in some applications. It is typically dark brown or grayish in color.

Other flour or cereal products not classified under specific categories. This includes flours derived from miscellaneous grains or processed forms not covered by other HS codes, such as specialty flours, bran, or partially processed grains.

Grain made from durum wheat, a hard wheat variety with high protein content. It is typically used for pasta, couscous, and semolina. The grain is pale yellow, dense, and has a distinct texture that supports the structural integrity of pasta products.

Grain made from common wheat and spelt, both cereal grains with moderate protein content. This grain is used in breads, pastries, and baked goods requiring a balanced texture and flavor. It is typically pale yellow or light beige in color.

Coarse or whole grain flour with fat content not exceeding 1.5% by weight. This flour is used in baking and food processing where fat content must be controlled. It is typically made from whole wheat or other cereal grains and has a textured, slightly gritty appearance.

Other coarse or whole grain flour not classified under specific categories. This includes flours made from miscellaneous grains or processed forms not covered by other HS codes, such as bran flours or whole grain blends.

Grain made from rye or barley, both cereal grains with distinct flavors and textures. Rye grain is dark and robust, while barley grain is pale and slightly sweet. Both are used in breads, soups, and traditional foods across various cultures.

Grain made from oats, a cereal grain rich in soluble fiber. Oat grain is light and creamy in texture, often used in baking, smoothies, and as a gluten-free alternative. It has a mild, slightly sweet flavor and is commonly used in oatmeal-based products.

Grain made from rice, a staple grain in many cultures. Rice grain is fine and white, gluten-free, and used in cakes, noodles, and gluten-free baking. It has a neutral flavor and smooth texture, commonly processed from long-grain or short-grain rice.

Grain or other cereal products not classified under specific categories. This includes grains derived from miscellaneous cereal varieties or processed forms not covered by other HS codes, such as specialty grains or bran.

Pellets made from rye or barley, cereal grains used in animal feed, food processing, or industrial applications. These pellets are compressed and have a uniform shape and size, suitable for storage, transport, and controlled release of nutrients.
Pellets made from oats, a cereal grain rich in soluble fiber. These pellets are compressed and uniform in shape, used in animal feed, food processing, or as a convenient form of oat-based nutrition.

Pellets made from maize (corn), a cereal grain with high starch content. These pellets are compressed and uniform in shape, used in animal feed, food processing, or as a convenient form of corn-based nutrition.
Pellets made from rice, a staple grain in many cultures. These pellets are compressed and uniform in shape, used in animal feed, food processing, or as a convenient form of rice-based nutrition.

Pellets made from wheat, a cereal grain with high protein content. These pellets are compressed and uniform in shape, used in animal feed, food processing, or as a convenient form of wheat-based nutrition.

Pellets made from other cereal grains not classified under specific categories. This includes pellets derived from miscellaneous grains or processed forms not covered by other HS codes, such as specialty grains or bran.

Rolled oats, a processed form of oat grain. These are flattened and slightly expanded, used in breakfast cereals, baking, and as a gluten-free alternative. They have a light, creamy texture and mild, slightly sweet flavor.

Flaked oats, a processed form of oat grain. These are thin, flat, and slightly expanded, used in breakfast cereals, baking, and as a gluten-free alternative. They have a light, creamy texture and mild, slightly sweet flavor.

Rolled wheat grain, a processed form of wheat. These are flattened and slightly expanded, used in breakfast cereals, baking, and as a gluten-free alternative. They have a light, creamy texture and mild, slightly sweet flavor.
Rolled rye grain, a processed form of rye. These are flattened and slightly expanded, used in breakfast cereals, baking, and as a gluten-free alternative. They have a dark, robust texture and flavor.

Rolled maize (corn) grain, a processed form of maize. These are flattened and slightly expanded, used in breakfast cereals, baking, and as a gluten-free alternative. They have a light, creamy texture and mild, slightly sweet flavor.

Rolled grain, a processed form of cereal grain. These are flattened and slightly expanded, used in breakfast cereals, baking, and as a gluten-free alternative. They have a light, creamy texture and mild, slightly sweet flavor.

Flaked grain, a processed form of cereal grain. These are thin, flat, and slightly expanded, used in breakfast cereals, baking, and as a gluten-free alternative. They have a light, creamy texture and mild, slightly sweet flavor.

Flaked rice, a processed form of rice. These are thin, flat, and slightly expanded, used in breakfast cereals, baking, and as a gluten-free alternative. They have a light, creamy texture and neutral flavor.

Other grain or cereal products not classified under specific categories. This includes grains derived from miscellaneous cereal varieties or processed forms not covered by other HS codes, such as specialty grains or bran.

Hulled (shelled or husked) oats, whether sliced or kibbled. These oats are processed to remove the outer husk, leaving the inner grain exposed. They are commonly used in animal feed, food processing, or as a convenient form of oat-based nutrition.

Pearled oats, a processed form of oat grain. These are polished or rounded to remove the outer husk, leaving the inner grain exposed. They are commonly used in animal feed, food processing, or as a convenient form of oat-based nutrition.

Other oat or cereal products not classified under specific categories. This includes oats derived from miscellaneous cereal varieties or processed forms not covered by other HS codes, such as specialty grains or bran.

Hulled (shelled or husked) maize (corn), whether sliced or kibbled; or pearled maize (corn). These are processed to remove the outer husk or polished to remove the outer layer, leaving the inner grain exposed. They are commonly used in animal feed, food processing, or as a convenient form of corn-based nutrition.

Other maize (corn) products not classified under specific categories. This includes maize (corn) derived from miscellaneous cereal varieties or processed forms not covered by other HS codes, such as specialty grains or bran.

Hulled (shelled or husked) grain, whether sliced or kibbled. These grains are processed to remove the outer husk, leaving the inner grain exposed. They are commonly used in animal feed, food processing, or as a convenient form of grain-based nutrition.

Pearled grain, a processed form of cereal grain. These are polished or rounded to remove the outer husk, leaving the inner grain exposed. They are commonly used in animal feed, food processing, or as a convenient form of grain-based nutrition.

Unprocessed cereal grains, excluding specific categories such as hulled, pearled, or ground forms. These are raw, whole grains in their natural state, typically used for direct consumption or further processing, including wheat, rye, barley, oats, and other cereal species not specified elsewhere.

Hulled cereal grains, whether sliced or crushed into smaller pieces. These grains have had their outer husks removed, and may be further processed into flakes, pellets, or granules, commonly used in animal feed or as raw materials for food preparation.

Pearled cereal grains, specifically processed to have a rounded, polished appearance. This treatment removes outer layers without complete hulling, improving texture and cooking time, often used in breakfast cereals or as a refined ingredient in food products.

Unprocessed wheat grains, whole and intact, without any additional processing such as hulling or pearling. These are typically used for milling into flour, animal feed, or as raw material in food and beverage production.

Unprocessed rye grains, whole and unaltered, preserved in their natural state. These are primarily used for bread-making, animal feed, or as raw material in food and beverage industries, especially in traditional European cuisines.

Unprocessed cereal grains of unspecified types, including but not limited to barley, oats, millet, or sorghum. These are raw, whole grains used for direct consumption, animal feed, or further processing into flour, meal, or food products.

Unprocessed wheat grains, whole and unprocessed, suitable for milling into flour or used as raw material for food and feed. These grains are typically used in bread-making, pasta production, or as a base for animal nutrition.

Unprocessed rye grains, whole and unaltered, commonly used for making rye bread, animal feed, or as a raw material in food and beverage industries. These grains are often used in traditional European or Scandinavian cuisines.

Unprocessed cereal grains of unspecified types, including barley, oats, millet, sorghum, or other cereal species not otherwise specified. These are raw, whole grains used for direct consumption, animal feed, or further processing into food products.

Flour, meal, or powder made exclusively from wheat. This includes finely ground wheat products used in baking, food processing, or animal feed, such as all-purpose flour, whole wheat flour, or wheat germ meal.

Flour, meal, or powder derived from other cereal grains besides wheat, including rye, barley, oats, millet, sorghum, or corn. These are used in baking, food processing, or animal feed, and may vary in texture and nutritional content depending on the source grain.

Flour, meal, or powder derived from potatoes, typically used in baking, food processing, or as a thickening agent. This includes finely ground potato products used in recipes, animal feed, or industrial applications such as starch production.

Potato flakes, granules, or pellets, typically used in food processing or as a raw material in the production of instant foods, soups, or animal feed. These are dried, processed forms of potato with varying textures and moisture content.

Flour or meal derived from dried leguminous vegetables classified under Chapter 0713, such as dried peas, beans, or lentils. These are used in food preparation, animal feed, or as a source of protein in human diets.
Denatured milk powder, chemically treated to prevent fermentation and spoilage. This product is commonly used in food processing, infant formula, or as a stabilizer in dairy products and beverages.

Milk powder or grain products not classified elsewhere, including skimmed, whole, or partially processed milk powders. These are used in food preparation, infant formula, or as a raw material in dairy and beverage production.

Flour or meal derived from bananas, typically used in food preparation, baking, or as a raw material in processed foods. These products may be dried, ground, or processed into powder for culinary or industrial applications.

Flour or meal derived from other fruits or vegetables not otherwise specified, including processed or dried forms. These are used in food preparation, animal feed, or as a raw material in food and beverage production.

Malt in the form of flour, ground from malted cereal grains such as barley. This is used in brewing, baking, or as a flavoring agent in food and beverage production, typically providing a sweet, nutty flavor and fermentable sugars.

Malt in any form not classified as flour, including flakes, pellets, or powder. This includes malted cereal grains processed for use in brewing, baking, or as a flavoring agent in food and beverage production.

Malt in the form of flour, ground from malted cereal grains such as barley. This is used in brewing, baking, or as a flavoring agent in food and beverage production, typically providing a sweet, nutty flavor and fermentable sugars.

Malt in any form not classified as flour, including flakes, pellets, or powder. This includes malted cereal grains processed for use in brewing, baking, or as a flavoring agent in food and beverage production.

Roasted malt, typically made from barley, processed by heating to develop flavor and aroma. This is used in brewing, baking, or as a flavoring agent in food and beverage production, enhancing taste and color.

Starch derived from wheat, extracted and purified for use in food, pharmaceutical, or industrial applications. This includes wheat starch used as a thickening agent, binder, or stabilizer in processed foods and beverages.

Starch derived from maize (corn), extracted and purified for use in food, pharmaceutical, or industrial applications. This includes corn starch used as a thickening agent, binder, or stabilizer in processed foods and beverages.

Starch derived from potatoes, extracted and purified for use in food, pharmaceutical, or industrial applications. This includes potato starch used as a thickening agent, binder, or stabilizer in processed foods and beverages.

Starch derived from manioc (cassava), extracted and purified for use in food, pharmaceutical, or industrial applications. This includes cassava starch used as a thickening agent, binder, or stabilizer in processed foods and beverages.

Starch derived from rice, extracted and purified for use in food, pharmaceutical, or industrial applications. This includes rice starch used as a thickening agent, binder, or stabilizer in processed foods and beverages.

Starch derived from other sources not otherwise specified, including tapioca, soy, or other plant-based sources. These are used as thickeners, stabilizers, or binders in food, pharmaceutical, or industrial applications.

Inulin, a naturally occurring polysaccharide extracted from plants such as chicory root. This is used as a prebiotic fiber, sweetener, or stabilizer in food, pharmaceutical, or industrial applications.
Wheat gluten, a protein-rich component extracted from wheat, either dried or undried. This is used in food preparation, animal feed, or as a binding agent in processed foods and beverages.

Whole seeds of safflower, intended for planting or propagation, typically used in agricultural settings to cultivate safflower for dye or oil production. These seeds are unprocessed and retain their natural outer husk, commonly harvested from the Carthamus tinctorius plant.

Other unspecified seeds not falling under specific categories, typically including seeds used for ornamental, experimental, or non-commercial purposes. These may be unprocessed or in bulk, intended for planting or research rather than immediate commercial use.

Whole seeds of safflower, intended for planting or propagation, typically used in agricultural settings to cultivate safflower for dye or oil production. These seeds are unprocessed and retain their natural outer husk, commonly harvested from the Carthamus tinctorius plant.

Safflower seeds still enclosed in their natural husks or shells, unprocessed and unshelled. These are typically harvested from the Carthamus tinctorius plant and may be used for planting, ornamental purposes, or as a raw material for oil extraction.

Safflower seeds that have been shelled, whether or not broken into fragments. These are processed for easier handling, planting, or extraction of oil. The shells are removed, leaving only the edible or usable kernel portion of the seed.

Copra, the dried kernel of the coconut, typically used for extraction of coconut oil. It is produced by drying the coconut meat after removal of the husk and is commonly processed for industrial or culinary uses.

Whole barley seeds intended for planting or propagation. These are typically used in agriculture to cultivate barley for grain, animal feed, or brewing. The seeds are unprocessed and retain their natural outer husk.

Other barley seeds not intended for planting, such as those used for animal feed, milling, or industrial processing. These may be processed or unprocessed and are typically not used for propagation purposes.

Whole seeds of ryegrass and similar grasses, intended for planting or propagation. These are commonly used in agriculture to establish turf, forage, or erosion control. The seeds are unprocessed and retain their natural husk.

Other ryegrass seeds not intended for planting, such as those used for animal feed, milling, or industrial processing. These may be processed or unprocessed and are typically not used for propagation purposes.

Other grass seeds not falling under specific categories, such as those used for ornamental, experimental, or non-commercial purposes. These may be unprocessed or in bulk, intended for planting or research rather than immediate commercial use.

Whole seeds of sunflower, intended for planting or propagation. These are typically used in agriculture to cultivate sunflowers for oil, seeds, or ornamental purposes. The seeds are unprocessed and retain their natural outer husk.

Sunflower seeds that are either shelled or remain in their grey-and-white striped shells. These are processed for easier handling, planting, or extraction of oil. The shells may be removed or left intact depending on intended use.

Other sunflower seeds not falling under specific categories, such as those used for animal feed, milling, or industrial processing. These may be processed or unprocessed and are typically not used for propagation purposes.

Palm nuts and kernels, typically harvested from oil palm trees. These are used for extraction of palm oil and may be processed or unprocessed. The kernels are the edible or usable portion of the fruit, often dried or roasted for various applications.

Whole seeds of various plants intended for planting or propagation. These may include seeds of ornamental, agricultural, or experimental plants. The seeds are unprocessed and retain their natural husks for planting purposes.

Other seeds not falling under specific categories, such as those used for animal feed, milling, or industrial processing. These may be processed or unprocessed and are typically not used for propagation purposes.

Castor oil seeds, harvested from the Ricinus communis plant. These are used for extraction of castor oil, which has industrial and medicinal applications. The seeds are typically unprocessed and retain their natural outer husk.

Whole seeds of various plants intended for planting or propagation. These may include seeds of ornamental, agricultural, or experimental plants. The seeds are unprocessed and retain their natural husks for planting purposes.

Other seeds not falling under specific categories, such as those used for animal feed, milling, or industrial processing. These may be processed or unprocessed and are typically not used for propagation purposes.

Whole seeds of various plants intended for planting or propagation. These may include seeds of ornamental, agricultural, or experimental plants. The seeds are unprocessed and retain their natural husks for planting purposes.

Other seeds not falling under specific categories, such as those used for animal feed, milling, or industrial processing. These may be processed or unprocessed and are typically not used for propagation purposes.

Safflower seeds, specifically from the Carthamus tinctorius plant. These are used for extraction of safflower dye or oil. The seeds are typically unprocessed and retain their natural outer husk, intended for planting or industrial use.

Melon seeds, typically harvested from the Cucumis melo plant. These are used for planting, animal feed, or culinary purposes. The seeds are unprocessed and retain their natural outer husk, often used in agriculture or food industries.

Whole seeds of various plants intended for planting or propagation. These may include seeds of ornamental, agricultural, or experimental plants. The seeds are unprocessed and retain their natural husks for planting purposes.

Other seeds not falling under specific categories, such as those used for animal feed, milling, or industrial processing. These may be processed or unprocessed and are typically not used for propagation purposes.

Whole seeds of various plants intended for planting or propagation. These may include seeds of ornamental, agricultural, or experimental plants. The seeds are unprocessed and retain their natural husks for planting purposes.

Hemp seeds, specifically from the Cannabis sativa plant. These are used for planting, animal feed, or culinary purposes. The seeds are unprocessed and retain their natural outer husk, often used in agriculture or food industries.

Other seeds not falling under specific categories, such as those used for animal feed, milling, or industrial processing. These may be processed or unprocessed and are typically not used for propagation purposes.

Milled soybeans, typically processed from the Glycine max plant. These are used for animal feed, food products, or industrial applications. The milling process breaks down the beans into a powder or flour form for easier handling.

Other oilseed meals or byproducts not falling under specific categories, such as those used for animal feed, milling, or industrial processing. These may be processed or unprocessed and are typically not used for propagation purposes.

Sugar beet seeds, specifically from the Beta vulgaris plant. These are used for planting or propagation of sugar beet crops. The seeds are unprocessed and retain their natural outer husk, typically used in agriculture for sugar production.

Lucerne seeds, specifically from the Trifolium alexandrinum plant. These are used for planting or propagation of lucerne crops. The seeds are unprocessed and retain their natural outer husk, typically used in agriculture for forage or animal feed.

Red clover seeds, specifically from the Trifolium pratense plant. These are used for planting or propagation of red clover crops. The seeds are unprocessed and retain their natural outer husk, typically used in agriculture for forage or animal feed.

Other clover seeds not falling under specific categories, such as those used for animal feed, milling, or industrial processing. These may be processed or unprocessed and are typically not used for propagation purposes.

Meadow fescue seeds, specifically from the Festuca pratensis plant. These are used for planting or propagation of meadow fescue crops. The seeds are unprocessed and retain their natural outer husk, typically used in agriculture for forage or erosion control.

Red fescue seeds, specifically from the Festuca rubra plant. These are used for planting or propagation of red fescue crops. The seeds are unprocessed and retain their natural outer husk, typically used in agriculture for forage or erosion control.

Other grass seeds not falling under specific categories, such as those used for animal feed, milling, or industrial processing. These may be processed or unprocessed and are typically not used for propagation purposes.

Kentucky bluegrass seeds, specifically from the Poa pratensis plant. These are used for planting or propagation of Kentucky bluegrass crops. The seeds are unprocessed and retain their natural outer husk, typically used in agriculture for turf or erosion control.

Italian ryegrass seeds, specifically from the Lolium multiflorum plant. These are used for planting or propagation of Italian ryegrass crops. The seeds are unprocessed and retain their natural outer husk, typically used in agriculture for forage or erosion control.

Perennial ryegrass (Lolium perenne L.) seed, typically used for pasture or lawn establishment, harvested as dry seeds, often sown in temperate regions for erosion control or livestock feed, with high germination rates and rapid growth characteristics.

Seeds of Timothy grass (Poa spp.), vetch, cocksfoot (Dactylis glomerata), and bent grass (Agrostis spp.), commonly used for livestock fodder or turfgrass mixes, with high nutritional value and adaptability to temperate climates, often sown as pure stands or in blends.

Lupine seed, primarily cultivated for forage or as a protein-rich crop for livestock, containing high levels of nitrogen and protein, with varieties adapted to dry or semi-arid conditions, typically harvested as dry, hard seeds.

Fodder beet seed (Beta vulgaris var. alba), cultivated for high sugar content and root yield, used as animal feed or forage, harvested as dry seeds, with varieties selected for early maturity and high biomass production.

Miscellaneous seed types not classified elsewhere, including ornamental, experimental, or hybrid seeds, often used for ecological restoration, specialty crops, or seed banks, not covered by more specific subheadings.

Seeds of herbaceous flowering plants, cultivated primarily for ornamental purposes, including annuals and perennials, such as marigolds, zinnias, or cosmos, often harvested as dry seeds for propagation or horticultural use.

Salad beet seed (Beta vulgaris var. conditiva), used for leafy greens or root vegetables, typically harvested as dry seeds, with varieties selected for high sugar content and crisp texture, often grown in cool-season climates.
Other vegetable seeds not classified elsewhere, including root, tuber, or leafy vegetable seeds, such as radish or turnip, often used for human consumption or animal feed, harvested as dry seeds.

Seeds of forest trees, including conifers and broadleaf species, harvested for reforestation, timber production, or ecological restoration, often collected from mature trees or seed orchards, not covered by other headings.

Other hop-related materials not classified elsewhere, including residual hop waste, byproducts, or unprocessed hop materials, often used in animal feed or composting, not covered by more specific subheadings.

Coca leaf, harvested from the plant Erythroxylum coca, used for traditional or medicinal purposes, typically dried and processed for export, with varieties adapted to tropical or subtropical climates.
Poppy straw, dried stems and stalks of the opium poppy (Papaver somniferum), used for fiber or as a raw material for pharmaceutical extraction, typically harvested as dry, unprocessed plant material.

Ephedra, harvested from the plant Ephedra sinica or related species, used for medicinal or dietary supplements, typically dried and processed for export, with varieties adapted to arid or semi-arid climates.

Bark of African cherry (Prunus africana), harvested from trees native to Africa, used for medicinal purposes, typically dried and processed for export, with varieties adapted to tropical or subtropical climates.

Tonquin beans, harvested from the plant Canavalia ensiformis, used for medicinal or nutritional purposes, typically dried and processed for export, with varieties adapted to tropical or subtropical climates.

Other unspecified plant materials not classified elsewhere, including aromatic or medicinal herbs, typically harvested as dried or processed materials for export, not covered by more specific subheadings.
Algae suitable for human consumption, including edible seaweeds or microalgae, typically harvested as fresh, dried, or processed forms, used for nutritional supplements or food products.
Other algae not classified elsewhere, including non-edible or experimental algae, typically harvested as dried or processed forms, used for research, industrial applications, or non-food purposes.
Dried or ground seeds of plants such as carob or locust beans, used for human consumption or animal feed, typically harvested as dry seeds, with varieties selected for high protein or fiber content.
Other dried or ground seeds not classified elsewhere, including experimental or hybrid seeds, typically harvested as dry seeds for human or animal use, not covered by more specific subheadings.

Locust beans (carob), harvested from the plant Ceratonia siliqua, used for human consumption or animal feed, typically dried and processed for export, with varieties selected for high fiber or low allergen content.

Sugar cane, harvested as whole stalks or processed into sugar, used for human consumption or industrial production, typically harvested as fresh or dried stalks, with varieties adapted to tropical climates.

Chicory roots, harvested from the plant Cichorium intybus, used for human consumption or animal feed, typically dried and processed for export, with varieties selected for high inulin or caffeine content.

Unprocessed seeds of sugar, taro, or other root crops, with intact shells, not crushed or ground, typically harvested as dry seeds for human or animal consumption, with varieties selected for high yield or flavor.

Other unspecified seeds of sugar, taro, or root crops, not covered by preceding subheadings, typically harvested as dry seeds for human or animal consumption, with varieties selected for high yield or flavor.

Other unspecified root or tuber materials not classified elsewhere, including experimental or hybrid varieties, typically harvested as fresh or dried materials for human or animal use, not covered by more specific subheadings.

Cereal straw and husks, unprocessed or minimally processed, including chopped, ground, pressed, or pelletized forms, typically harvested as dry materials for animal bedding, erosion control, or biofuel production.

Lucerne (alfalfa) meal and pellets, processed from harvested alfalfa, used as animal feed or fertilizer, typically dried and ground into powder or compressed into pellets, with varieties selected for high protein content.
Mangolds, swedes, and other fodder roots, harvested as whole roots or processed into feed, typically dried or ground for animal consumption, with varieties selected for high sugar or fiber content.

Other unspecified fodder materials not classified elsewhere, including root, tuber, or leafy vegetable materials, typically harvested as fresh or dried materials for animal feed or composting, not covered by more specific subheadings.

Gum arabic, a natural gum derived from the sap of Acacia trees, primarily used as a stabilizer, emulsifier, or thickener in food, pharmaceuticals, and cosmetics. It appears as a fine, yellowish powder or granular form, often dried and processed for industrial applications.

Opium, a resinous substance extracted from the unripe seed pods of the opium poppy (Papaver somniferum), containing alkaloids such as morphine and codeine. It is used in medicine, pharmaceutical production, and as a raw material for illicit drug manufacturing.

Liquorice extract, derived from the root of Glycyrrhiza glabra, containing glycyrrhizin. It is used as a flavoring agent, sweetener, and additive in food, beverages, and pharmaceuticals, often appearing as a dark brown or black viscous liquid or paste.

Hop extract, obtained from the cones of Humulus lupulus, used primarily as a flavoring and preservative in beer production. It may also be used in pharmaceuticals and as a natural insecticide, appearing as a dark brown or black liquid or powder.

Ephedra extract, derived from the plant Ephedra sinica or related species, containing ephedrine and pseudoephedrine. It is used in traditional medicine and as a stimulant, typically appearing as a dark brown or black liquid or powder.

Vanilla oleoresin, a concentrated extract obtained from vanilla beans (Vanilla planifolia), containing vanillin and other aromatic compounds. It is used as a flavoring agent in food, beverages, and perfumery, typically appearing as a dark brown, viscous liquid.

Other plant extracts, including those from various botanical sources not specified elsewhere in this chapter, used in food, pharmaceutical, or cosmetic industries. These may appear as liquids, powders, or pastes, depending on extraction method and application.

Dry pectin, a water-soluble polysaccharide extracted from plant cell walls, particularly from citrus fruits and apples. It is used as a gelling agent in food production, appearing as a white or light-colored powder or granules.

Other pectin, including modified or specialty forms not classified under dry pectin, used as gelling agents in food, pharmaceuticals, or cosmetics. May appear as powders, granules, or liquid concentrates depending on processing.

Agar-agar, a gelatinous substance derived from red algae (mainly species of Gelidium and Gracilaria). It is used as a thickener and gelling agent in food, pharmaceuticals, and microbiology, appearing as a white or light-colored powder or flakes.

Locust bean gum, extracted from the seeds of the carob tree (Ceratonia siliqua) or the pods of the locust tree (Robinia pseudoacacia). It is used as a thickener and stabilizer in food, cosmetics, and pharmaceuticals, appearing as a light brown or white powder.

Guar gum, derived from the seeds of the guar plant (Cyamopsis tetragonophylla). It is used as a thickener and stabilizer in food, pharmaceuticals, and industrial applications, appearing as a white or light-colored powder or granules.

Other plant gums and extracts, including those derived from sources not specified elsewhere in this chapter, used as thickeners, stabilizers, or emulsifiers in food, pharmaceuticals, or cosmetics. May appear as powders, granules, or liquids.

Bamboo, including raw, processed, or finished forms, typically used in furniture, flooring, or crafts. May be solid, laminated, or woven. Includes both green and dried bamboo, regardless of treatment, as long as it remains in its natural plant form.

Rattan, including whole stems or processed strips, used for weaving furniture, baskets, or decorative items. Typically includes Calamus and Daemonorops species. May be dried, split, or partially processed for crafting.

Other grasses or reeds, including cane, reed, or similar fibrous plants, not classified elsewhere. May be raw, processed, or used in crafts, construction, or decorative applications. Typically includes species like Miscanthus or Phragmites.

Cotton linters, fine short fibers extracted from cotton seeds. Used primarily in manufacturing of explosives, paper, or specialty textiles. Not classified as cotton yarn or fabric, and not processed into staple fiber form.

Other plant fibers or materials, including seed fluff or other non-staple plant byproducts. Typically not used in textile production. May include cottonseed hair or similar fibrous residues from processing.

Cocoa beans, whole or broken, raw or roasted, are the edible seeds of the cacao tree. They are typically used in chocolate production and may be processed through roasting to enhance flavor. These beans are unprocessed or minimally treated before further refining into cocoa products.

Cocoa shells, husks, skins, and other waste materials from cocoa bean processing. These byproducts are typically discarded after extraction but may be used for composting, biofuel, or as animal feed. They consist of fibrous, dry outer layers of the cocoa bean.

Unrefined cocoa paste, containing no fat removed, is a thick, moist mass derived from ground cocoa beans. It retains natural cocoa solids and fat, and is used as an ingredient in chocolate production or as a flavoring agent in food products.

Defatted cocoa paste, either wholly or partly, has had a portion of its cocoa butter removed. This product is used in chocolate manufacturing to control fat content and is often blended with other ingredients to achieve desired texture and flavor.

Cocoa butter, fat, and oil are solid fats extracted from cocoa beans. They are primarily used in chocolate to provide texture and mouthfeel, and are also used in cosmetics and pharmaceuticals. Cocoa butter is a natural emulsifier and has a high melting point.

Cocoa powder, unsweetened, is finely ground cocoa solids with no added sugar or sweeteners. It is used in baking, beverages, and confectionery to impart chocolate flavor and color. The powder is typically alkalized to reduce acidity.

Cocoa powder with less than 5% sucrose, including invert sugar or isoglucose expressed as sucrose, is unsweetened or lightly sweetened. It is used in recipes where minimal sugar content is desired, such as in dietary or health-conscious products.

Cocoa powder containing 5% to 65% sucrose, including invert sugar or isoglucose expressed as sucrose, is moderately sweetened. It is commonly used in chocolate products and beverages where balanced sweetness is required without excessive sugar.

Cocoa powder with 65% to 80% sucrose, including invert sugar or isoglucose expressed as sucrose, is highly sweetened. This product is often used in commercial confectionery or in recipes requiring concentrated sweetness and flavor intensity.

Cocoa powder with 80% or more sucrose, including invert sugar or isoglucose expressed as sucrose, is extremely sweetened. It is used in specialty confections, beverages, or industrial applications where intense sweetness is desired.

Chocolate products weighing more than 2 kg, including blocks, bars, or liquid forms, are typically packaged for bulk or commercial sale. These may include chocolate in paste, powder, or molded forms, and are often used in industrial or retail settings.

Chocolate products with at least 31% cocoa butter or combined cocoa butter and milkfat, weighing over 2 kg, are high-fat chocolate products. They are often used in premium confections or in products requiring a rich, creamy texture.

Chocolate products with combined cocoa butter and milkfat content between 25% and 31%, weighing over 2 kg, are mid-fat chocolate products. These are commonly used in standard chocolate bars or confections requiring moderate richness.

Chocolate products with at least 18% cocoa butter, weighing over 2 kg, are low-fat chocolate products. They are often used in lighter confections or in products designed for health-conscious consumers.

Chocolate milk crumb is a granular, powdered form of chocolate milk. It is used as an ingredient in baked goods, beverages, or as a topping, and is typically blended with milk or other dairy products to create a chocolate flavor.

Chocolate flavor coating is a thin, edible layer applied to food items for flavor enhancement. It is commonly used in candies, confections, or as a decorative finish on baked goods and is typically made from cocoa butter and sugar.
