Other derivatives of malonylurea (barbituric acid), including substituted barbiturates or salts thereof, used as anticonvulsants or in pharmaceutical formulations.
Loprazolam (INN), mecloqualone (INN), methaqualone (INN), and zipeprol (INN), along with their salts, used as sedative-hypnotics or anticonvulsants in medical applications.
Diazinon (ISO), an organophosphate insecticide used for pest control in agriculture, households, and public health applications.
1,4-Diazabicyclo[2.2.2]octane (triethylenediamine), a diamine compound used as a chemical intermediate, solvent, or corrosion inhibitor in industrial or pharmaceutical applications.

Other chemical substances not specified elsewhere in Chapter 29, including miscellaneous organic or inorganic compounds not falling under specific categories, typically used in industrial or research applications.
Melamine, a nitrogen-rich heterocyclic compound used as a chemical intermediate, in resins, or as a food additive (in some countries), typically appearing as a white crystalline solid.

Atrazine (ISO), propazine (ISO), simazine (ISO), and hexahydro-1,3,5-trinitro-1,3,5-triazine (hexogen, trimethylenetrinitramine), used as herbicides or explosive compounds in agriculture or military applications.

Methenamine (INN) (hexamethylenetetramine), used as a urinary antiseptic or chemical intermediate; 2,6-di-tert-butyl-4-[4,6-bis(octylthio)-1,3,5-triazin-2-ylamino]phenol, a synthetic compound used in research or specialized chemical formulations.
Other chemical substances not specified elsewhere in Chapter 29, including miscellaneous triazine derivatives or related compounds not classified under specific INN codes.
6-Hexanlactam (epsilon-caprolactam), a cyclic amide used primarily as a monomer in the production of nylon-6, typically appearing as a colorless liquid or crystalline solid.
Clobazam and methyprylon, both benzodiazepine-related compounds, used as sedatives and anticonvulsants, typically supplied as pure chemicals or pharmaceutical intermediates.
Other lactams not specified elsewhere, including cyclic amides with a carbonyl group and nitrogen, used in polymer synthesis or as chemical intermediates.
Chlordiazepoxide, a benzodiazepine derivative, used as a sedative and anxiolytic, typically supplied as a pure compound or in pharmaceutical formulations.
Other chemical substances not classified elsewhere in this heading, including unspecified pharmaceuticals or industrial chemicals.
Azinphos-methyl, an organophosphate insecticide, used in agriculture, typically appearing as a colorless liquid or crystalline solid.
Indole and its derivatives, including skatole, azapetine, phenindamine, and imipramine hydrochloride, used in pharmaceuticals, fragrances, or agrochemicals.
2,4-Di-tert-butyl-6-(5-chlorobenzotriazol-2-yl)phenol, a UV stabilizer used in polymers and coatings, typically supplied as a colorless solid.
Other unspecified chemical substances, including miscellaneous pharmaceuticals or industrial intermediates not covered elsewhere.
Chemical compounds containing an unfused thiazole ring, whether hydrogenated or not, used in pharmaceuticals or as intermediates for synthetic chemistry.
Di(benzothiazol-2-yl)disulfide and benzothiazole-2-thiol (mercaptobenzothiazole), used as antioxidants and vulcanization accelerators in rubber, typically supplied as solids or liquids.
Other chemical substances not specified elsewhere in this heading, including unspecified intermediates or specialty chemicals.
Thiethylperazine and thioridazine, antipsychotic drugs used in psychiatric treatment, typically supplied as pure compounds or salts.
Other chemical substances not specified elsewhere in this heading, including unspecified pharmaceuticals or intermediates.

Pharmaceutical substances including aminorex, brotizolam, clotiazepam, dextromoramide, ketazolam, mesocarb, oxazolam, pemolin, phendimetrazin, phenmetrazin, and sufentanil, typically supplied as pure compounds or salts.
Other fentanyl derivatives and their chemical compounds, including semi-synthetic or synthetic analogs used in pain management or research.
Chlorprothixene, thenalidine, furazolidone, 7-aminocephalosporanic acid, and related salts or esters, used as antiparasitics, antiseptics, or antibiotics.
Other unspecified chemical substances, including miscellaneous pharmaceuticals or agrochemicals not covered elsewhere.
N-Methylperfluorooctane sulphonamide, a fluorinated surfactant or intermediate used in industrial or pharmaceutical applications, typically a colorless solid.
N-Ethylperfluorooctane sulphonamide, a fluorinated compound used in surfactants or as a chemical intermediate, typically supplied as a solid or liquid.
N-Ethyl-N-(2-hydroxyethyl)perfluorooctane sulphonamide, a fluorinated surfactant derivative, used in specialty applications or as a chemical intermediate.
N-(2-Hydroxyethyl)-N-methylperfluorooctane sulphonamide, a fluorinated compound used in surfactants or as an intermediate in chemical synthesis.
Other perfluorooctane sulphonamides, including unspecified derivatives used in industrial or pharmaceutical applications.
Metosulam, a sulfonylurea herbicide, used in agriculture to control broadleaf weeds, typically supplied as a crystalline solid.
Other unspecified chemical substances, including miscellaneous herbicides or intermediates not covered elsewhere.
Vitamin A and its derivatives, including retinol and retinal, used in nutrition, pharmaceuticals, or as additives in food or cosmetics.
Vitamin B1 (thiamine) and its derivatives, used in nutritional supplements, pharmaceuticals, or as additives in food or feed.
Vitamin B2 (riboflavin) and its derivatives, used in nutritional supplements, pharmaceuticals, or as food additives.
D- or DL-Pantothenic acid (vitamin B5) and its derivatives, used in nutritional supplements, pharmaceuticals, or as food additives.
Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) and its derivatives, used in nutritional supplements, pharmaceuticals, or as food additives.
Vitamin B12 and its derivatives, used in nutritional supplements, pharmaceuticals, or as additives in animal feed.
Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) and its derivatives, used in nutritional supplements, pharmaceuticals, or as food additives.
Vitamin E and its derivatives, used in nutritional supplements, pharmaceuticals, or as antioxidants in food or cosmetics.
Other vitamins and their derivatives, including unspecified fat-soluble or water-soluble vitamins for pharmaceutical or nutritional use.
Other chemical substances not specified elsewhere, including natural concentrates such as plant extracts or essential oils.
Somatotropin (growth hormone) and its derivatives or structural analogs, used in medicine for hormone replacement or growth promotion.
Insulin and its salts, used in the treatment of diabetes, typically supplied as purified pharmaceuticals for injection.
Other hormone-related substances, including unspecified endocrine or metabolic regulators.
Corticosteroid hormones including cortisone, hydrocortisone, prednisone, and prednisolone, used in anti-inflammatory or immunosuppressive treatments.
Halogenated derivatives of corticosteroid hormones, used in pharmaceuticals for enhanced stability or potency.

Oestrogens and progestogens are synthetic or naturally occurring hormones used in pharmaceuticals for hormonal regulation. These include compounds like estradiol and progesterone, typically formulated as powders, tablets, or injectables for medical use in contraception, menopause treatment, or hormone replacement therapy.

Other hormonal substances not classified elsewhere, including miscellaneous endocrine regulators and synthetic analogues used in medical or research contexts. These may include non-standard derivatives or structural analogues not falling under specific prostaglandin or steroid categories.

Prostaglandins, thromboxanes, and leukotrienes, along with their chemical derivatives and structural analogues, are lipid-based signaling molecules involved in inflammation, blood clotting, and smooth muscle contraction. These are typically used in pharmaceuticals, veterinary medicine, or research settings.

Other chemical substances not classified under specific hormonal or prostaglandin categories, including miscellaneous organic compounds used in pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, or industrial applications where no other HS code applies.

Rutoside (rutin) and its derivatives are flavonoid compounds with antioxidant properties. They are commonly used in pharmaceuticals, dietary supplements, or topical preparations for vascular health and skin care, typically formulated as powders or tablets.

Digitalis glycosides are cardiac glycosides derived from plants like foxglove. Used in medicine to treat heart failure and arrhythmias, they are typically formulated as tablets, powders, or injectables with precise dosing to manage cardiac function.

Glycyrrhizic acid and its derivatives are compounds extracted from licorice root. Used in pharmaceuticals and food additives for their anti-inflammatory and flavoring properties, often found in syrups, tablets, or topical formulations.

Other medicinal plant-derived compounds not classified elsewhere, including miscellaneous alkaloids, glycosides, or extracts used in traditional or modern medicine, typically in tablet, powder, or liquid forms.

Concentrates of poppy straw and narcotic alkaloids such as morphine, codeine, heroin, and oxycodone. These are typically used in pharmaceuticals for pain management and are subject to strict regulatory controls due to their addictive potential.

Other opium alkaloids not classified under specific narcotic compounds, including miscellaneous derivatives and alkaloids extracted from opium poppy, typically used in research or controlled pharmaceutical applications.

Cinchona alkaloids, including quinine and quinidine, along with their derivatives and salts. Used historically for malaria treatment and in cardiovascular medicine, typically formulated as tablets, powders, or injectables.

Caffeine and its salts are central nervous system stimulants used in pharmaceuticals, beverages, and dietary supplements. Typically found in tablets, powders, or liquid formulations for alertness and energy enhancement.

Ephedrine and its salts are stimulant alkaloids derived from ephedra plants. Used in pharmaceuticals for bronchodilation and weight loss, typically formulated as tablets, powders, or liquid preparations for respiratory or metabolic support.

Pseudoephedrine (INN) and its salts are decongestants used to relieve nasal congestion. Typically formulated as tablets, powders, or liquid forms for respiratory conditions, often regulated due to potential misuse.

Cathine (INN) and its salts are stimulant alkaloids found in khat plants. Used in some traditional medicines and dietary supplements for alertness and appetite suppression, typically formulated as powders or tablets.

Norephedrine and its salts are stimulant alkaloids chemically related to ephedrine. Used in pharmaceuticals for respiratory and circulatory support, typically formulated as tablets or powders with controlled dosing.

Levometamfetamine, metamfetamine (INN), and their racemates and salts are potent central nervous system stimulants. Used in some medical contexts for narcolepsy and weight loss, typically formulated as tablets or powders with strict regulatory controls.

Other plant-derived alkaloids not classified elsewhere, including miscellaneous stimulants or medicinal compounds extracted from plants, typically used in pharmaceuticals or research settings with regulatory oversight.

Fenetylline (INN) and its salts are stimulant alkaloids used in some pharmaceuticals for appetite suppression and weight loss. Typically formulated as tablets or powders with controlled dosing and regulatory restrictions.

Other aminophylline compounds, including derivatives of theophylline and caffeine, used in bronchodilators for asthma and respiratory conditions. Typically found in tablets, powders, or liquid formulations for inhalation or oral use.

Ergometrin (INN) and its salts are uterotonic agents used in obstetrics to control postpartum bleeding. Typically formulated as tablets or injectables for medical use under strict regulatory controls.
Ergotamine (INN) and its salts are vasospasm inhibitors used in migraine treatment. Typically formulated as tablets or powders for oral or sublingual administration with controlled medical use.

Lysergic acid and its salts are psychedelic alkaloids derived from ergot fungus. Used in research, medicine, or controlled settings for their psychoactive and therapeutic properties, typically in powder or tablet forms.
Other medicinal plant-derived alkaloids not classified elsewhere, including miscellaneous stimulants, sedatives, or compounds used in traditional or modern medicine with regulatory oversight.

Cocaine and ecgonine, along with their salts, esters, and derivatives, are potent central nervous system stimulants. Used in medicine for local anesthesia and in research, typically with strict regulatory controls due to their addictive potential.

Nicotine and its salts, ethers, esters, and derivatives are alkaloids from tobacco plants. Used in pharmaceuticals for smoking cessation and in nicotine replacement therapies, typically in patches, gum, or liquid forms.
Other medicinal plant-derived alkaloids not classified elsewhere, including miscellaneous stimulants, sedatives, or compounds used in traditional or modern medicine with regulatory oversight.
Other organic compounds not classified elsewhere, including miscellaneous plant-derived or synthetic compounds used in pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, or research settings with regulatory controls.

Chemically pure sugars excluding sucrose, lactose, maltose, glucose, and fructose; sugar ethers, acetals, esters, and their salts. Used in pharmaceuticals, food additives, or industrial applications with precise chemical purity requirements.

Penicillins and their derivatives with penicillanic acid structure, including antibiotics used to treat bacterial infections. Typically formulated as powders, tablets, or injectables with broad-spectrum activity against gram-positive bacteria.

Dihydrostreptomycin and its salts, esters, and hydrates are aminoglycoside antibiotics used to treat gram-negative bacterial infections. Typically formulated as powders or injectables for systemic or topical use.
Other antibiotics not classified elsewhere, including miscellaneous antimicrobial agents used in research or medical applications with regulatory oversight.

Tetracyclines and their derivatives are broad-spectrum antibiotics used to treat a wide range of bacterial infections. Typically formulated as tablets, powders, or injectables with varying pharmacokinetic profiles.

Chloramphenicol and its derivatives are broad-spectrum antibiotics used to treat bacterial infections, especially in cases where other antibiotics are contraindicated. Typically formulated as powders, tablets, or injectables.

Erythromycin and its derivatives are macrolide antibiotics used to treat gram-positive and some gram-negative bacterial infections. Typically formulated as tablets, powders, or injectables with good tissue penetration.
Other antibiotics not classified elsewhere, including miscellaneous antimicrobial agents used in research or medical applications with regulatory oversight.
Other organic compounds not classified elsewhere, including miscellaneous pharmaceutical or industrial chemicals used in research, medicine, or cosmetics with regulatory controls.

Human-derived sera, typically obtained from blood donations, used for medical purposes such as diagnostic testing or therapeutic applications. These products are not processed into specific fractions or purified forms and retain their original biological composition.

Sera not of human origin, including those derived from animals, used for research, diagnostic, or therapeutic purposes. These may include serum from livestock or other species, not processed into specific fractions or purified components.

Human-derived sera, specifically prepared for medical use, including diagnostic or therapeutic applications. These are not processed into specific fractions or purified components and retain their natural composition.
Heparin and its salts, used as anticoagulants in medical treatments. These are typically derived from animal sources such as porcine or bovine tissues, purified for pharmaceutical use.
Other human-derived sera not classified elsewhere, including those used for diagnostic, therapeutic, or research purposes. These may contain unprocessed or minimally processed biological components.

Antisera and other blood fractions, including immunoglobulins and coagulation factors, used for therapeutic or diagnostic purposes. These are derived from human blood and processed to isolate specific components.
Unmixed immunological products, such as antibodies or antigens, not prepared in measured doses or retail packaging. These are typically used in laboratory or clinical research settings.
Mixed immunological products, such as antibody cocktails or antigen mixtures, not prepared in measured doses or retail packaging. These are used for research or diagnostic purposes.
Immunological products prepared in measured doses or retail packaging, including vaccines or antibody therapies. These are intended for direct medical use by patients or healthcare providers.
Vaccines specifically targeting SARS-related coronaviruses, including SARS-CoV-1 and SARS-CoV-2. These are formulated for human use and may include live attenuated, inactivated, or subunit vaccines.
Other vaccines not classified elsewhere, including those for viral, bacterial, or parasitic diseases. These may be for human or veterinary use and not specified under other categories.
Vaccines intended for veterinary use, including those for livestock or companion animals. These are formulated to prevent or treat animal-specific diseases and are not for human use.
Other microbial cultures, including bacterial, fungal, or viral strains used for research, diagnostic, or industrial purposes. These are not classified elsewhere and may be in liquid or solid form.

Cell therapy products, including stem cells, immune cells, or genetically modified cells used for therapeutic purposes. These are typically prepared for clinical use and may involve autologous or allogeneic sources.
Other biological products not classified elsewhere, including recombinant proteins, gene therapies, or biologics used for therapeutic or diagnostic purposes.
Human blood, typically collected from donors, used for transfusion, diagnostic, or research purposes. This includes whole blood and may be stored in liquid or frozen form.
Animal blood prepared for therapeutic, prophylactic, or diagnostic use. This includes blood from livestock or other animals processed for medical applications, such as antiserum or plasma.
Other biological products not classified elsewhere, including blood derivatives or unprocessed biological materials used for medical or research purposes.

Medicines containing penicillins or derivatives with penicillanic acid structure, or streptomycins or derivatives. These are used for treating bacterial infections and may include antibiotics for systemic or topical use.
Other medicines containing antibiotics, including broad-spectrum or narrow-spectrum agents. These are used for treating bacterial infections and may be in oral, injectable, or topical forms.
Medicines containing insulin, used for managing diabetes. These are typically formulated for injection or oral use and may include human or animal-derived insulin.
Other medicines not classified elsewhere, including those with unspecified active ingredients or formulations. These may be used for general therapeutic purposes.
Medicines containing ephedrine or its salts, used for treating respiratory conditions or as stimulants. These are typically formulated for oral or inhalation use.

Medicines containing norephedrine or its salts, used for treating respiratory conditions or as stimulants. These are typically formulated for oral or inhalation use.
Other medicines not classified elsewhere, including those with unspecified active ingredients or formulations. These may be used for general therapeutic purposes.

Medicines containing antimalarial active principles as specified in Subheading Note 2, including quinine, chloroquine, or artemisinin derivatives. These are used for treating malaria and may be in oral or injectable forms.

Medicines containing penicillins or derivatives with penicillanic acid structure, or streptomycins or derivatives. These are used for treating bacterial infections and may include antibiotics for systemic or topical use.

Medicines containing insulin, used for managing diabetes. These are typically formulated for injection or oral use and may include human or animal-derived insulin.

Medicines containing corticosteroid hormones, their derivatives, or structural analogues. These are used for treating inflammatory, autoimmune, or allergic conditions and may be in oral, injectable, or topical forms.
Other medicines not classified elsewhere, including those with unspecified active ingredients or formulations. These may be used for general therapeutic purposes.
Medicines containing ephedrine or its salts, used for treating respiratory conditions or as stimulants. These are typically formulated for oral or inhalation use.
Medicines containing pseudoephedrine (INN) or its salts, used for decongestion and treating nasal congestion. These are typically formulated for oral use.
Medicines containing norephedrine or its salts, used for treating respiratory conditions or as stimulants. These are typically formulated for oral or inhalation use.
Other medicines not classified elsewhere, including those with unspecified active ingredients or formulations. These may be used for general therapeutic purposes.

Medicines containing vitamins or other products classified under heading 2936, including vitamin supplements or fortified formulations. These are typically used for nutritional or therapeutic purposes.

Medicines containing antimalarial active principles as specified in Subheading Note 2, including quinine, chloroquine, or artemisinin derivatives. These are used for treating malaria and may be in oral or injectable forms.
Other medicines not classified elsewhere, including those with unspecified active ingredients or formulations. These may be used for general therapeutic purposes.

Adhesive dressings and other medical articles with an adhesive layer, typically made of flexible, sterile materials like film or cloth, designed for wound protection or securing medical devices on the body, often used in first aid or surgical settings.

Wadding and articles made of wadding, typically soft, absorbent materials like cotton or synthetic fibers, used for cushioning, padding, or as protective layers in medical dressings, surgical pads, or wound care applications.

Gauze and articles made from gauze, a woven or non-woven fabric often sterile, used for wound dressings, surgical drapes, or as absorbent materials in medical and first aid contexts, typically lightweight and breathable.
Other articles made of wadding or gauze-like materials, including non-gauze pads, compresses, or dressings, used in medical or veterinary settings for wound care, drainage, or as protective barriers.
Other unspecified articles made of wadding or gauze-like materials, including non-standard forms, composite dressings, or modified medical textiles used for wound management or surgical support.

Sterile surgical catgut, a resorbable suture material derived from animal collagen, used for internal wound closure in surgery, designed to degrade naturally in the body over time without requiring removal.

Sterile surgical or dental adhesion barriers, including both absorbable and non-absorbable types, used to prevent tissue adhesion or contamination during surgical or dental procedures, often applied to wounds or implant sites.

Other pharmaceutical articles not specified elsewhere, including sterile solutions, ointments, or powders used in medical procedures, typically for wound care, infection control, or systemic administration.

Opacifying preparations for X-ray examinations, including contrast agents such as iodine or barium compounds, and diagnostic reagents designed for intravenous or oral administration to enhance imaging visibility.

Dental cements and bone reconstruction cements, including materials like zinc phosphate, glass ionomer, or acrylic-based compounds, used for tooth restoration, dental fillings, or bone grafting in surgical procedures.

First-aid boxes and kits, typically pre-assembled containers with essential medical supplies like bandages, antiseptics, and tools, designed for emergency use in households, workplaces, or public facilities.
Chemical contraceptive preparations, including hormonal pills, patches, or gels, as well as spermicidal agents or products classified under heading 2937, designed for human use to prevent pregnancy.

Gel preparations for medical or veterinary use, including lubricants or coupling agents applied to body parts during surgery or examinations, designed to reduce friction or improve instrument contact.
Appliances identifiable for ostomy use, including specialized pouches, barriers, or connectors designed for patients with ostomies, to manage waste and protect surrounding skin.
Waste pharmaceuticals, including expired, damaged, or unused medicinal products, typically collected for disposal or destruction to prevent environmental or public health risks.

Placebos and blinded clinical trial kits, including pre-measured doses of inactive substances or controlled packaging, designed for use in recognized clinical trials to ensure trial integrity and patient blinding.

Fertilizers derived from animal or vegetable sources, including blends or chemically treated forms. These may include composted manures, organic extracts, or processed plant residues. Used primarily in agriculture to enhance soil fertility and crop yield. Products may be granular, powdered, or liquid.

Urea-based nitrogen fertilizers with more than 45% nitrogen by weight (dry, anhydrous basis). This includes pure urea or urea concentrates used to supply nitrogen to crops. Typically appears as white crystalline solids and is widely used in agriculture due to its high nitrogen content and ease of application.

Other nitrogen fertilizers not specifically classified elsewhere. Includes synthetic or natural nitrogen compounds not covered by urea or ammonium salts. May include ammonium nitrate, nitric acid derivatives, or other nitrogen-rich substances used for crop nutrition.

Ammonium sulfate, a nitrogen and sulfur-containing fertilizer. Typically appears as white crystals or granules. Used to supply nitrogen and sulfur to crops, especially in soils deficient in these nutrients. Common in vegetable and cereal production.

Other nitrogen fertilizers not specifically listed. Includes ammonium nitrate, ammonium chloride, or other ammonium-based salts not covered by specific codes. May be granular, powdered, or liquid, depending on formulation and application method.

Nitrogen fertilizers dissolved in water. Includes liquid nitrogen solutions such as ammonium nitrate solutions or urea in water. Used for foliar spraying or drip irrigation. Typically applied directly to plant surfaces or soil for immediate nutrient uptake.

Other nitrogen fertilizers not in aqueous solution. Includes solid or gaseous forms such as ammonium nitrate granules, urea prills, or ammonium bicarbonate. Used in conventional soil applications or specialized crop systems.

Nitrogen fertilizers with nitrogen content not exceeding 28% by weight. Includes ammonium sulfate, ammonium chloride, or other low-nitrogen ammonium salts. Used for crops requiring moderate nitrogen levels or where high-nitrogen fertilizers are not suitable.

Nitrogen fertilizers with nitrogen content exceeding 28% by weight. Includes ammonium nitrate, urea, or other high-nitrogen compounds. Used for intensive crop production or soils requiring high nitrogen availability.

Sodium nitrate, a nitrogen-rich fertilizer containing 16% nitrogen by weight. Commonly used in arid regions or for specific crops such as beets and potatoes. Typically appears as white crystals or granules and is soluble in water.

Double salts or mixtures of calcium nitrate and ammonium nitrate. These provide both calcium and nitrogen, suitable for soils lacking these nutrients. Often used in greenhouse or specialty crop production.

Mixtures of urea and ammonium nitrate dissolved in water or ammoniacal solutions. These are liquid fertilizers designed for foliar feeding or soil application. Commonly used in horticulture and intensive crop systems.

Other nitrogen fertilizers not specified elsewhere. Includes complex blends, organic nitrogen sources, or novel formulations. May include liquid or solid forms and are used for specialized agricultural applications.

Phosphorus fertilizers with diphosphorus pentaoxide (P2O5) content exceeding 35% by weight. Includes concentrated phosphoric acid or phosphates such as monoammonium phosphate. Used to supply phosphorus to crops during early growth stages.

Other phosphorus fertilizers not specifically classified. Includes phosphoric acid, rock phosphate, or phosphorus-containing salts not covered by specific codes. Used in soils deficient in phosphorus for root and flower development.

Other phosphorus fertilizers not specified elsewhere. Includes organic phosphates, phosphorus-based blends, or modified phosphates. Used in various agricultural applications, including soil amendments and plant nutrition.

Potassium fertilizers with potassium content (as K2O) not exceeding 40% by weight. Includes potassium chloride, potassium sulfate, or potassium nitrate. Used to enhance plant growth and stress resistance, particularly in fruit and vegetable crops.

Potassium fertilizers with potassium content (as K2O) exceeding 40% but not exceeding 62% by weight. Includes high-potassium salts like potassium sulfate or potassium nitrate. Used for crops requiring high potassium levels, such as potatoes or tomatoes.

Potassium fertilizers with potassium content (as K2O) exceeding 62% by weight. Includes concentrated potassium salts like potassium chloride or potassium sulfate. Used in intensive agriculture or for crops requiring high potassium availability.

Potassium sulfate, a potassium and sulfur-containing fertilizer. Typically appears as white crystals or granules. Used to supply potassium and sulfur to crops, especially in soils deficient in these nutrients. Common in fruit and vegetable production.

Other potassium fertilizers not specified elsewhere. Includes potassium nitrate, potassium chloride, or potassium-based blends. Used in various agricultural applications, including soil amendments and plant nutrition.

Fertilizers in tablet or small-package forms (gross weight ≤ 10 kg). Includes slow-release formulations or granular fertilizers packaged for convenience. Used for targeted application or in controlled-release systems.

Nitrogen-containing blends with nitrogen content exceeding 10% by weight (dry basis). Includes ammonium nitrate blends, urea blends, or other nitrogen-rich compounds. Used for balanced nutrient delivery in agriculture.

Other nitrogen-containing blends not specified elsewhere. Includes complex blends, organic-nitrogen fertilizers, or modified formulations. Used in specialized agricultural applications or for crop-specific nutrient requirements.

Diammonium phosphate, a nitrogen and phosphorus fertilizer. Contains approximately 18% nitrogen and 46% phosphorus (as P2O5). Commonly used in cereal and vegetable production for balanced nutrient supply.

Monoammonium phosphate, a nitrogen and phosphorus fertilizer. Often blended with diammonium phosphate. Contains approximately 11% nitrogen and 52% phosphorus (as P2O5). Used in soils requiring balanced nitrogen-phosphorus supply.

Fertilizers containing both nitrates and phosphates. Includes blends of ammonium nitrate and diammonium phosphate or other combined formulations. Used to provide dual nutrient supply for crops requiring both nitrogen and phosphorus.

Other fertilizers not specified elsewhere. Includes complex blends, organic fertilizers, or modified formulations. Used in specialized agricultural applications or for crop-specific nutrient requirements.

Mineral or chemical fertilizers containing both phosphorus and potassium. Includes blends such as potassium phosphate or potassium nitrate with phosphorus. Used to supply both nutrients for balanced crop nutrition.

Nitrogen-containing blends with nitrogen content exceeding 10% by weight (dry basis). Includes ammonium nitrate blends, urea blends, or other nitrogen-rich compounds. Used for balanced nutrient delivery in agriculture.

Other nitrogen-containing blends not specified elsewhere. Includes complex blends, organic-nitrogen fertilizers, or modified formulations. Used in specialized agricultural applications or for crop-specific nutrient requirements.

Extracts derived from quebracho trees, used primarily as natural tannins in leather processing. These extracts are typically brownish liquids, rich in tannic acid, and employed to tan animal hides. They may contain varying proportions of gallotannins and ellagitannins, and are often used as alternatives to synthetic tanning agents.

Extracts from wattle trees, specifically mimosa species, used in the tanning industry. These are natural tannins, often yellow to brown in color, and contain hydrolysable tannins. Used to treat hides, they provide durable leather and are commonly sourced from Australia or other regions with native wattle species.

Natural tannin extracts from sumach, valonea, oak, or chestnut trees. These are used in leather tanning and dyeing. Sumach extract is reddish-brown, valonea is yellowish, oak is dark brown, and chestnut is light brown. They are often used as partial substitutes for quebracho or wattle extracts.

Other tannin extracts not specified elsewhere, including those from various plant sources. These may be derived from bark, leaves, or fruits and are used for tanning hides, dyeing, or as stabilizers. They are typically liquid or semi-solid and may vary in color and chemical composition.

Synthetic organic tanning agents, including aromatic and aliphatic compounds, designed to replace or supplement natural tannins. These are often used in industrial leather processing for efficiency, consistency, and reduced environmental impact. They may be water-soluble or require special solvents for application.

Other tanning agents not classified elsewhere, including both synthetic and natural compounds. These may be used in leather tanning, wood preservation, or as stabilizers. They are typically liquid or solid and may vary in chemical structure, solubility, and application method.

Vegetable-based colorants and preparations used in dyeing and coloring textiles or leather. These include natural pigments such as curcumin, annatto, or indigo, often extracted from plants. Preparations may include mordants or carriers to improve colorfastness.

Animal-derived colorants and preparations, including cochineal, carmine, or bone black. These are used for dyeing textiles, leather, or paints. Preparations may include mordants or carriers to enhance color intensity and durability.

Disperse dyes and their preparations, designed for dyeing synthetic fibers like polyester. These are typically water-soluble and require dispersing agents to remain stable in solution. Used in textile manufacturing for coloration of synthetic fabrics.

Acid dyes and mordant dyes, used for dyeing protein-based fibers such as wool or silk. Acid dyes require acidic conditions for proper binding, while mordant dyes use metal salts to fix color. These are often used in high-quality textile dyeing processes.

Basic dyes, typically used for dyeing synthetic fibers. These are water-soluble and often have high brightness. Used in textile dyeing, especially for polyester or acrylic fibers, they may require specific fixing agents for optimal results.

Direct dyes, which can be applied directly to fibers without mordants. These are water-soluble and often used for dyeing cotton, silk, and wool. They are applied under controlled conditions and are commonly used in textile dyeing industries.

Vat dyes, which require reduction to a soluble form before application and then oxidation to fix the color. These are used for dyeing cotton, wool, and silk. Often used in high-quality textile dyeing, they offer excellent colorfastness.

Reactive dyes, which form covalent bonds with fibers. These are used for dyeing cotton, wool, and silk. They require specific conditions for application and are known for excellent colorfastness and wash resistance.

Pigments and preparations based on pigments, used for coloring inks, paints, and plastics. These include organic and inorganic pigments, often finely ground, and may be dispersed in various solvents or binders.

Carotenoid-based colorants and their preparations, used for coloring food, plastics, and textiles. These are naturally derived from plants and offer yellow to red hues. Often used in food coloring and cosmetic applications.

Other colorants, including mixtures of dyes or pigments from different subheadings. These may be used for specialized applications such as printing, dyeing, or coating, and often require specific formulations for compatibility.

Synthetic organic fluorescent brightening agents, used to enhance the whiteness or brightness of materials. These are often added to paper, textiles, or plastics to improve visual appearance under light. They are typically water-soluble and may require specific solvents.

Other colorants not specified elsewhere, including specialty dyes or pigments. These may be used for industrial, artistic, or commercial applications. They may include blends or modified formulations for specific uses.

Colour lakes, which are pigments formed by precipitating dyes with metal salts. These are used in paints, inks, and plastics for vibrant colors. Preparations based on colour lakes are often more stable and colorfast than pure dyes.

Pigments containing 80% or more titanium dioxide by weight, calculated on dry matter. Titanium dioxide is a white pigment used in paints, coatings, and plastics. These pigments are highly opaque and provide excellent lightfastness.

Other pigments not specified elsewhere, including those with varying titanium dioxide content. These may be used in paints, inks, or plastics for coloration or opacity. They may include other white or light-colored pigments.

Pigments and preparations based on chromium compounds, including chromium oxide and chromium yellow. These are used in paints, inks, and plastics for their vibrant colors and durability. Chromium compounds are often used in industrial applications.

Ultramarine pigments and their preparations, derived from natural or synthetic sources. These are blue pigments used in paints, inks, and plastics. Ultramarine is known for its intense blue color and is often used in artistic and industrial applications.

Lithopone and other pigments based on zinc sulfide, used for their opacity and color stability. Lithopone is white and used in paints, inks, and plastics. These pigments are often used in industrial applications where opacity is required.

Magnetite pigments, a naturally occurring iron oxide. These are black or dark brown pigments used in paints, inks, and plastics. Magnetite is often used for its magnetic properties and color stability.

Other pigments not specified elsewhere, including those with varying chemical compositions. These may be used in paints, inks, or plastics for coloration or opacity. They may include other inorganic or organic pigments.

Inorganic luminophores, used to emit light when exposed to radiation. These are often used in fluorescent paints, inks, or plastics. They may include phosphors or other materials that emit light under specific conditions.

Prepared pigments, opacifiers, and prepared colors for paints, inks, or plastics. These include ground pigments, dispersions, and formulations designed for specific applications. They may include binders, solvents, or additives.

Engobes, or slips, are liquid clay suspensions used in ceramics. These are applied to pottery to create decorative or protective coatings. Engobes may contain pigments or glazes and are applied before firing.

Other ceramic coatings or slips not specified elsewhere, including decorative or protective coatings. These may be used in pottery, tiles, or other ceramic applications. They may include binders, solvents, or additives.

Liquid lustres and similar preparations used in ceramics or plastics. These are typically metallic or pearlescent in appearance and are used to enhance the visual appeal of finished products. They may include binders or solvents.

Glass in the form of flakes or powder with specific dimensional and chemical specifications. These flakes are typically 0.1–3.5 mm long and 2–5 micrometers thick. Powdered glass with 99% or more silicon dioxide is used in coatings, insulation, or specialty applications.

Other glass materials not specified elsewhere, including glass powders, granules, or flakes. These may be used in coatings, insulation, or specialty applications. They may include various types of glass with different chemical compositions.

Solutions as defined in note 4 to this chapter, used for coating or finishing materials. These may include solvents, resins, or additives designed for specific applications. They may be used in paints, inks, or plastics.

Other coating or finishing materials not specified elsewhere, including solutions or formulations designed for specific applications. These may be used in paints, inks, or plastics. They may include binders, solvents, or additives.

Solutions as defined in note 4 to this chapter, used for coating or finishing materials. These may include solvents, resins, or additives designed for specific applications. They may be used in paints, inks, or plastics.

Other coating or finishing materials not specified elsewhere, including solutions or formulations designed for specific applications. These may be used in paints, inks, or plastics. They may include binders, solvents, or additives.

Polyurethane resin based on 2,2'- (tert-butylimino) diethanol and 4,4'-methylene dicyclohexyl diisocyanate, dissolved in N,N-dimethylacetamide with 48% or more polymer content. Used as a coating or adhesive, it offers high durability and flexibility.

Copolymer of p-cresol and divinylbenzene dissolved in N,N-dimethylacetamide with 48% or more polymer content. Used as a coating or adhesive, it offers high durability and flexibility. Often used in industrial or commercial applications.
Paints and varnishes not falling under other specified categories, including those based on natural or synthetic materials not detailed elsewhere in this chapter.
Paints and varnishes formulated with synthetic polymers, including acrylics, polyurethanes, or vinyls, used for industrial, architectural, or decorative applications.
Paints and varnishes based on chemically modified natural polymers such as casein, alkyd, or modified cellulose, typically used for specialized coatings or traditional finishes.
Paints and varnishes based on acrylic or vinyl polymers, commonly used for durable, weather-resistant finishes in construction, automotive, and industrial applications.
Paints and varnishes not classified under specific polymer-based categories, including experimental or custom formulations not covered elsewhere in this chapter.
Oil-based paints and varnishes, including enamels and lacquers, derived from drying oils such as linseed or tung oil, used for artistic or protective coatings.
Paints and varnishes not classified under oil-based or synthetic polymer categories, including solvent-based or specialty formulations not otherwise specified.
Prepared driers used in paints and varnishes to accelerate drying, including metallic salts like cobalt or lead, often added as additives in industrial formulations.
Stamping foils used in printing or decorative applications, typically metallic or colored, applied to surfaces via mechanical pressure or heat.
Printing pastes not classified under stamping foils, including ink rollers, paste-type inks, or auxiliary materials used in printing or decorative arts.
Artists’ paints sold in pre-mixed sets, including acrylic, oil, or watercolor, designed for creative use and typically packaged for retail or educational purposes.
Artists’ paints not classified under pre-mixed sets, including individual tubes, jars, or specialty pigments used for artistic or experimental purposes.
Glaziers’ putty, grafting putty, resin cements, and caulking compounds used for sealing, filling, or joining surfaces in construction or restoration applications.
Painters’ fillers used for patching or covering imperfections in surfaces, typically applied by hand or brush, often containing pigment and binder.
Other materials used as fillers or sealants not classified under specific types of putty or caulking compounds, including specialized mastics or adhesives.
Black ink used for writing, printing, or marking, including liquid, paste, or solid forms suitable for pens, printers, or industrial applications.
Ink used for non-black applications, including colored inks or specialty formulations not otherwise specified in this chapter.
Ink cartridges without integrated print heads, designed for insertion into printers under specified subheadings, including mechanical or electrical components.
Other ink products not classified under specific types, including refillable inks, ink sticks, or custom formulations not otherwise specified.

Unrefined orange oil, derived from the peels of Citrus sinensis, containing natural terpenes and esters, used primarily in perfumery and flavoring applications, with no prior chemical extraction or purification.

Refined orange oil, processed to remove impurities and volatile components, resulting in a concentrated, odorless or less aromatic product suitable for pharmaceutical, cosmetic, and food-grade applications.
Unrefined citron oil, extracted from Citrus medica or similar citrus species, retaining natural terpenes and limonene, used in perfumery, aromatherapy, and as a flavoring agent in food and beverages.

Refined citron oil, chemically treated to remove impurities and volatile compounds, resulting in a purified, stable product used in cosmetics, pharmaceuticals, and as a flavoring agent in food industries.

Unrefined plant oil, including essential oils from various plant sources, not subjected to any chemical or physical refinement, preserving natural constituents for use in perfumery, aromatherapy, and food.
Refined plant oil, chemically or physically treated to remove impurities, resulting in a purified essential oil suitable for pharmaceutical, cosmetic, and food-grade applications.
Unrefined peppermint oil, extracted from Mentha × piperita, containing high levels of menthol and menthone, used in pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, and as a flavoring agent in food and beverages.
Refined peppermint oil, processed to remove impurities and volatile components, resulting in a concentrated, odorless or less aromatic product suitable for pharmaceutical and cosmetic applications.
Unrefined mint oil, derived from Mentha spp., containing natural terpenes and esters, used in perfumery, aromatherapy, and as a flavoring agent in food and beverages.
Refined mint oil, chemically treated to remove impurities and volatile compounds, resulting in a purified, stable product used in cosmetics, pharmaceuticals, and food industries.

Unrefined plant oil, including essential oils from various plant sources, not subjected to any chemical or physical refinement, preserving natural constituents for use in perfumery, aromatherapy, and food.
Refined plant oil, chemically or physically treated to remove impurities, resulting in a purified essential oil suitable for pharmaceutical, cosmetic, and food-grade applications.
Plant oil of rose origin, derived from Rosa spp., containing natural terpenes and aromatic compounds, used in perfumery, cosmetics, and as a flavoring agent in food and beverages.
Plant oil from other unspecified sources, including essential oils from various plant species, used in perfumery, aromatherapy, and as a flavoring agent in food and beverages.
Plant oil of geranium, jasmine, or vetiver root origin, containing natural terpenes and aromatic compounds, used in perfumery, cosmetics, and as a flavoring agent in food and beverages.
Lavender or lavandin oil, derived from Lavandula spp., containing natural terpenes and aromatic compounds, used in perfumery, aromatherapy, and as a flavoring agent in food and beverages.
Plant oil from other unspecified sources, including essential oils from various plant species, used in perfumery, aromatherapy, and as a flavoring agent in food and beverages.
Resinoids, synthetic or natural aromatic compounds derived from resins, used in perfumery, cosmetics, and as flavoring agents in food and beverages.
Terpenic by-products from the deterpenation of volatile vegetable oils, including natural terpenes and esters, used in perfumery, aromatherapy, and as flavoring agents in food and beverages.

Lakrids and hops oil, derived from Glycyrrhiza glabra and Humulus lupulus, containing natural terpenes and aromatic compounds, used in perfumery, aromatherapy, and as flavoring agents in food and beverages.
Plant oil from other unspecified sources, including essential oils from various plant species, used in perfumery, aromatherapy, and as a flavoring agent in food and beverages.
Plant oil from other unspecified sources, including essential oils from various plant species, used in perfumery, aromatherapy, and as a flavoring agent in food and beverages.
Flavoring or fragrance blends containing alcohol content exceeding 0.5% by volume, used in food, beverages, and cosmetics as flavoring or scenting agents.

Flavoring or fragrance blends containing no milk fat, sucrose, isoglucose, glucose, or starch, or containing less than specified thresholds of these ingredients by weight, used in food, beverages, and cosmetics.
Flavoring or fragrance blends from other unspecified sources, used in food, beverages, and cosmetics as flavoring or scenting agents.
Flavoring or fragrance blends from other unspecified sources, used in food, beverages, and cosmetics as flavoring or scenting agents.
Flavoring or fragrance blends specifically formulated for use in the food industry, including food-grade flavoring and scenting agents.
Alcoholic solutions of flavoring or fragrance compounds, used in food, beverages, and cosmetics as flavoring or scenting agents.
Flavoring or fragrance blends from other unspecified sources, used in food, beverages, and cosmetics as flavoring or scenting agents.
Perfumes, concentrated aromatic compounds derived from essential oils or synthetic materials, used for personal scenting and fragrance applications.
Toilet waters, diluted aromatic solutions used for personal hygiene and scenting, typically containing water, alcohol, and fragrance compounds.
Lip make-up preparations, including lipsticks, lip glosses, and balms, formulated with pigments, oils, and emollients for cosmetic use.
Eye make-up preparations, including eyeshadows, mascaras, and eyeliner, formulated with pigments, oils, and emollients for cosmetic use.
Manicure and pedicure preparations, including nail polishes, varnishes, and treatments, formulated with resins, pigments, and solvents for cosmetic use.
Powders for cosmetic use, including loose or compressed powders, formulated with pigments, talc, and other minerals for skin coverage or setting.
Other cosmetic preparations, including facial masks, creams, and treatments, formulated with various ingredients for skin care or cosmetic use.
Shampoos, liquid or gel-based hair washes containing surfactants, conditioning agents, and fragrances for cleansing and conditioning hair.
Preparations for permanent waving or straightening, including chemical agents and additives for altering hair structure and texture.
Hair lacquers, including hair sprays and gels, formulated with resins, solvents, and fragrances for styling and setting hair.
Other hair care preparations, including hair masks, conditioners, and treatments, formulated with various ingredients for hair care or cosmetic use.

Toothpaste and dental care products designed for oral hygiene, typically containing abrasive agents, fluoride, or flavoring, used to clean teeth and freshen breath. May include toothbrushes or pastes in tubes or jars, intended for daily personal use.

Dental floss, typically made of nylon or silk, designed for cleaning interdental spaces. Used to remove food particles and plaque between teeth, often sold in spools or waxed forms for convenient handling.
Other dental care products not specified elsewhere, including mouthwashes, tooth powders, or oral rinses, typically liquid or paste-based, intended for personal oral hygiene and breath freshness.

Pre-shave, shaving, and aftershave preparations designed for personal use. Includes creams, gels, or lotions containing alcohol, water, or oils to soften hair, lubricate skin, or soothe irritation after shaving.

Personal deodorants and antiperspirants designed to reduce body odor or prevent perspiration. May include roll-ons, sprays, or creams containing aluminum salts, fragrances, or alcohol for use on skin.

Perfumed bath salts and other bath preparations designed for personal use. Typically contain salts, essential oils, or fragrances to enhance bathing experience, often sold in containers or sachets for use in water.

Odoriferous preparations that operate by burning, such as agarbatti (incense sticks) or similar products. Typically made from resin, wood, or fragrant herbs, designed to release aroma when ignited for air purification or ritual use.
Other odoriferous preparations not specified elsewhere, including incense cones, aromatic oils, or burnable sachets, used for scenting air or ceremonial purposes and typically combustible in nature.
Other personal care products not specified elsewhere, including bath oils, massage oils, or skin care preparations, typically liquid or emollient-based, intended for personal hygiene or cosmetic use.

Soap intended for toilet use, including medicated varieties, typically solid or bar-shaped, used for personal hygiene in bathrooms. May contain added ingredients for therapeutic purposes such as antiseptics or moisturizers, and is generally manufactured for direct consumer use.

Soap not falling under other specified categories, including specialty or unconventional forms such as liquid soaps without added preservatives or medicated soaps not classified elsewhere. May include artisanal or niche formulations.

Soap in forms such as flakes, wafers, granules, or powders, typically used for industrial or commercial cleaning, or as bath or shower additives. May be formulated for easy dissolution or dispersion in water.
Soap in any form not specified elsewhere, including unique shapes, textures, or packaging. May include novelty or decorative soaps, or soaps with unusual additives such as fragrances or colorants.

Liquid or cream-based skin cleansing preparations containing organic surfactants, often packaged for retail sale. May include soap as an ingredient, and are typically used for bathing or personal hygiene, with or without added moisturizers or fragrances.

Linear alkylbenzene sulfonic acids and their salts, primarily used as surfactants in detergents and cleaning agents. These compounds are water-soluble and commonly found in household and industrial cleaning formulations.

Aqueous solutions containing 30% to 50% by weight of disodium alkyl [oxydi(benzenesulphonate)], a type of surfactant. Used in detergents and cleaning products for their foaming and emulsifying properties, often in commercial or industrial applications.

Other detergents and cleaning agents not specified elsewhere, including surfactant blends, specialty formulations, or those containing non-standard ingredients. May include eco-friendly or biodegradable alternatives.

Cationic surfactants, positively charged surface-active agents used in fabric softeners, hair conditioners, and disinfectants. These compounds are effective at reducing static and enhancing lubricity in textile and cosmetic applications.

Non-ionic surfactants, neutral surface-active agents used in detergents, emulsifiers, and cleaning products. These are less sensitive to water hardness and widely used in personal care and industrial cleaning formulations.

Other surfactants and surface-active agents not classified elsewhere, including specialty or modified types such as silicone-based or polymer-based surfactants. May be used in niche applications like industrial cleaning or pharmaceuticals.

Surface-active preparations, including emulsifiers, foaming agents, and dispersants, used in detergents, cosmetics, and industrial cleaning. These may be single or multi-component formulations, often designed for specific cleaning tasks.

Washing, cleaning, and degreasing preparations, including detergents, soaps, and degreasers, designed for household, industrial, or commercial use. May contain surfactants, enzymes, or solvents for enhanced cleaning performance.

Surface-active preparations, including surfactants and emulsifiers, used in detergents and cleaning agents. May include specialized formulations for specific applications such as industrial cleaning or cosmetic products.

Washing, cleaning, and degreasing preparations, including detergents, soaps, and degreasers, designed for household, industrial, or commercial use. May contain surfactants, enzymes, or solvents for enhanced cleaning performance.

Preparations for treating textile materials, leather, furskins, or other materials, typically used for waterproofing, stain resistance, or preservation. May include impregnating agents or coatings applied to fabrics or leather goods.

Preparations containing 70% or more by weight of petroleum oils or oils derived from bituminous minerals, used for impregnating materials but not as the primary component. May include lubricants or protective coatings for wood or textiles.

Lubricants with at least 25% bio-based carbon content and at least 60% biodegradability, used for impregnating materials. These are environmentally friendly alternatives designed for sustainable use in textiles or leather treatments.

Other impregnating preparations not specified elsewhere, including specialty formulations for specific materials or applications. May include water-based or solvent-based treatments for wood, leather, or textiles.

Preparations for treating textile materials, leather, furskins, or other materials, typically used for waterproofing, stain resistance, or preservation. May include impregnating agents or coatings applied to fabrics or leather goods.

Other impregnating preparations not specified elsewhere, including specialty formulations for specific materials or applications. May include water-based or solvent-based treatments for wood, leather, or textiles.

Waxes based on poly(oxyethylene) (polyethylene glycol), commonly used in cosmetics, pharmaceuticals, and industrial applications. These waxes are water-soluble and often used as binders or emulsifiers in formulations.

Other waxes not specified elsewhere, including natural or synthetic waxes such as beeswax, paraffin wax, or carnauba wax. May be used in cosmetics, food, or industrial applications for coating, sealing, or lubrication.

Polishes, creams, and similar preparations for treating footwear or leather goods. These may include conditioning agents, protective coatings, or cleaning agents designed to restore or maintain the appearance and durability of leather.

Polishes and similar preparations for maintaining wooden furniture, floors, or other woodwork. May include finishes, sealants, or protective coatings designed to enhance the appearance and protect wood surfaces from damage.

Polishes and similar preparations for coachwork, excluding metal polishes. These are typically used for restoring or maintaining the finish of painted or varnished surfaces on vehicles or other vehicles, often containing solvents and resins.

Scouring pastes, powders, and other abrasive cleaning preparations used for removing dirt, stains, or oxidation from surfaces. May include abrasives, detergents, or solvents designed for heavy-duty cleaning applications.

Polishes and similar preparations specifically designed for metal surfaces, including cleaning, polishing, or protective coatings. May include compounds that remove tarnish or oxidation, or provide corrosion resistance.

Other preparations not specified elsewhere, including specialty polishes, cleaning agents, or protective coatings for surfaces not covered under other categories. May include formulations for specific materials or applications.

Candles, tapers, and similar wax-based products, including those made from stearin, paraffin, or other waxes. May include decorative, functional, or ceremonial uses, often with added fragrances or colors.

Modelling pastes, including those for children's toys, dental wax, and impression materials for dental applications. May include gypsum-based formulations, plasters, or other materials shaped into specific forms for artistic or medical use.

Propellant powders, including black powder, used primarily in firearms and pyrotechnics. These are finely ground mixtures of oxidizers and fuels, typically containing potassium nitrate, charcoal, and sulfur. They generate rapid combustion to produce gas pressure for propulsion or ignition.

Prepared explosives excluding propellant powders, such as TNT, RDX, or nitroglycerin-based compounds. These are typically solid or semi-solid materials designed for detonation or deflagration in military, industrial, or demolition applications. Often packaged for controlled use.

Safety fuses designed to burn at a controlled, predictable rate to ignite or delay detonation. Made from combustible materials like cotton or paper impregnated with oxidizers. Used in detonators, fireworks, and military ordnance for safe handling and timing.

Detonating cords, flexible tubes filled with high explosives like PETN or RDX, designed to transmit detonation waves. Used in blasting operations and military applications to initiate detonation of other charges. Typically wrapped in protective sheathing for durability.

Percussion caps, small metal caps containing explosive material, typically fulminate of mercury or similar compounds. When struck, they detonate to ignite a primary charge. Commonly used in firearms and detonators for initiating explosions.

Detonating caps, also known as blasting caps, contain sensitive explosives designed to initiate detonation. They are often electrically or mechanically triggered and used in controlled detonation systems for mining, construction, or military applications.

Igniters, small devices designed to initiate combustion or detonation. May include friction, spark, or electrical ignition mechanisms. Used in fireworks, pyrotechnics, and industrial applications to start controlled burning or explosive reactions.

Electric detonators, devices that use electrical current to trigger detonation. Contain an electrically sensitive explosive charge and a firing circuit. Used in mining, construction, and military applications for precise, remote initiation of explosions.

Fireworks, including pyrotechnic compositions and devices designed to produce light, sound, or color effects. May contain explosive, oxidizing, and fuel materials, often packaged in casings or shells. Used in displays, celebrations, and theatrical effects.

Other pyrotechnic articles not covered elsewhere in Chapter 36, such as signal flares, smoke devices, or novelty fireworks. These may include combustible materials designed for controlled burning or explosion, often with specific ignition mechanisms.

Matches, excluding pyrotechnic articles under heading 3604. These include friction or strike-anywhere matches with combustible heads and sticks. Typically used for lighting fires, candles, or other flammable materials in everyday applications.

Liquid or liquefied-gas fuels in containers designed for filling or refilling cigarette or similar lighters. Capacity not exceeding 300 cm³. Includes fuels such as butane, propane, or naphtha, typically stored under pressure for portable use.

Ferro-cerium and other pyrophoric alloys in any form, including granules, rods, or powders. These alloys ignite spontaneously upon exposure to air and are used in lighters, flints, or industrial ignition systems.

Handmade paper and paperboard, crafted entirely by hand using traditional methods such as mold-and-paddle or deckle techniques. These products may include decorative or artistic papers with unique textures, fibers, or finishes. They are not machine-made and often feature irregular edges or natural fiber variations.

These substrates are typically coated or treated to support chemical or thermal reactions, such as in photographic film, thermal paper, or electronic displays. They are not intended for direct use but serve as foundational layers.

These products are mostly made from chemical pulp or a blend of chemical and semi-chemical pulp, used in applications requiring higher strength or specific surface properties. Paper and paperboard not classified under other specific categories, including those made from non-mechanical pulp or blends where mechanical pulp content exceeds 10% but does not meet other criteria. Warp pile fabrics, woven with pile yarns aligned parallel to the warp threads. These fabrics often feature a dense, upright pile and are used in carpets, rugs, and decorative textiles, sometimes incorporating synthetic or natural fibers for durability.

Garden or similar umbrellas, including parasols, designed for outdoor use. Typically constructed with a rigid or flexible frame and canopy, often made of fabric or synthetic materials. Umbrellas with telescopic shafts, designed for compact storage and portability. Typically feature collapsible frames that extend and retract smoothly, often made of metal or composite materials. Articles containing magnesite, magnesia (in the form of periclase, dolomite, or burned dolomite), or chromite, typically used in refractory materials, industrial abrasives, or construction applications due to their high heat resistance and structural strength.

Ceramic goods made from siliceous fossil materials such as diatomite, tripolite, or kieselguhr, or similar siliceous earths. These include bricks, blocks, tiles, and other ceramic products, typically fired for construction or industrial use, with high silica content and low thermal expansion.

Highly refractory stones containing over 50% by weight of magnesium oxide (MgO), calcium oxide (CaO), or chromium oxide (Cr2O3), either individually or collectively. These materials are used in extreme heat applications, such as furnace linings or metallurgical crucibles, due to their exceptional thermal stability.

Refractory stones with 93% or more silica (SiO2) by weight. These are highly resistant to thermal shock and chemical corrosion, typically used in furnace linings, kilns, and high-temperature industrial equipment where pure silica content ensures structural integrity under extreme conditions.

Refractory stones containing more than 7% but less than 45% alumina (Al2O3) by weight. These materials offer good thermal resistance and mechanical strength, suitable for applications like furnace components or refractory linings in metal processing where moderate alumina content enhances durability.

Refractory stones not classified under other specific categories. These may include various compositions with mixed refractory properties, such as those with moderate alumina, silica, or other oxides, used in general industrial applications requiring heat resistance.

Refractory stones not falling under other specified subheadings. This includes miscellaneous refractory materials with variable compositions, typically used in high-temperature applications such as kilns, furnaces, or industrial heat exchangers where specific oxide content is not defined.

Ceramic crucibles containing over 50% free carbon by weight. These are used for melting metals or chemicals at high temperatures, where carbon content provides thermal insulation and chemical inertness, particularly in metallurgy or laboratory applications.

Ceramic crucibles with less than 45% alumina (Al2O3) by weight. These are typically used for high-temperature applications where lower alumina content allows for more flexibility in thermal expansion and chemical resistance, often in metallurgy or chemical processing.

Ceramic crucibles with 45% or more alumina (Al2O3) by weight. These are highly resistant to thermal shock and chemical corrosion, commonly used in metallurgical furnaces or laboratory settings for melting metals and ceramics at elevated temperatures.

Ceramic crucibles containing 25% to 50% graphite or other carbon materials, either alone or mixed. These are used for high-temperature operations where carbon provides thermal insulation and chemical inertness, particularly for non-ferrous metals or specialized metallurgical processes.

Ceramic crucibles not classified under other specific categories. These may include crucibles with variable carbon or alumina content, used in specialized industrial or laboratory applications requiring thermal resistance without specific compositional criteria.

Building bricks made of ceramic material, typically fired clay or other refractory earths. These are used for structural purposes in walls and foundations, offering compressive strength and durability under load, often with a standardized shape and size.

Ceramic building materials not classified under other specific categories. These may include specialized bricks, blocks, or structural components used in construction, such as decorative or insulating bricks with varying compositions and uses.

Roofing tiles made of ceramic material, typically fired clay or other refractory earths. These are designed for weather resistance and durability, often with curved or interlocking shapes to provide waterproofing and structural integrity for roofing applications.

Ceramic roofing materials not classified under other specific categories. These may include decorative or specialized tiles, such as guttering, flashing, or roof vents, used in roofing applications where specific shapes or functions are not covered under other headings.

Ceramic pipes, conduits, guttering, and pipe fittings, typically made from fired clay or other refractory materials. These are designed for fluid transport, drainage, or ventilation, with durable, non-corrosive properties suitable for both residential and industrial applications.

Ceramic tiles with a water absorption coefficient by weight not exceeding 0.5%. These are highly dense and non-porous, used in applications requiring moisture resistance, such as flooring, walls, or exterior cladding in wet or humid environments.

Ceramic tiles with a water absorption coefficient by weight exceeding 0.5% but not exceeding 10%. These are moderately porous, suitable for indoor applications where some moisture resistance is needed but not for exterior use or high-moisture environments.

Ceramic tiles with a water absorption coefficient by weight exceeding 10%. These are highly porous and typically used in interior applications where moisture resistance is not critical, such as decorative or non-load-bearing wall coverings.

Ceramic mosaic tiles and similar products, excluding those classified under 690740. These are typically small, square or rectangular tiles used for decorative surfaces, often in floors or walls, with a mosaic-like appearance and varied glaze finishes.

Ceramic tiles intended for decorative purposes, such as wall or floor coverings with ornamental designs or finishes. These are often glazed or patterned for aesthetic appeal, used in interior design, architectural features, or artistic installations.

Ceramic articles made of porcelain or china, typically characterized by high density, low porosity, and high strength. These include tableware, sanitary ware, or decorative items with a smooth, glazed finish and high resistance to thermal and mechanical stress.

Ceramic articles with a hardness equivalent to 9 or more on the Mohs scale, indicating extreme durability and resistance to scratching. These are typically used in laboratory or industrial settings for precision instruments, cutting tools, or high-stress applications.

Ceramic articles made from other ceramic materials not classified under other specific categories. These may include specialized compositions with unique properties, used in industrial or scientific applications requiring specific mechanical or chemical characteristics.

Ceramic articles not classified under other specific categories. These may include miscellaneous ceramic products with variable compositions or uses, typically used in industrial, scientific, or decorative applications where specific classification is not applicable.

Ceramic tableware and kitchenware made from stoneware, typically fired at high temperatures for greater durability. These are used for heavy-duty applications, such as serving hot foods or in commercial kitchens, with dense, non-porous surfaces.
