Non-self-propelled hay balers designed to be towed or carried by tractors or other vehicles. These machines are typically mounted on trailers or frames and require external power for operation, suitable for small or medium-scale operations.

Hay balers without motors, typically manually operated or powered by human or animal labor. These machines are used in small-scale or traditional farming operations, often featuring simple mechanical systems for baling hay.

Hay balers with internal combustion engines designed for self-propelled operation. These machines are typically mounted on tractors or trailers and feature engines for power, suitable for medium to large-scale operations.

Hay balers designed to be mounted on or hauled by tractors. These machines are typically towed or carried by tractors and feature hitching mechanisms for easy attachment, suitable for field-based operations.

Other hay balers not covered in preceding categories, including specialized or niche models. These may be used for specific applications, such as hay collection in confined spaces or for specific crop types.

Other haymaking machinery not covered in preceding categories, including specialized equipment for hay collection, drying, or storage. These machines may vary in design and application depending on field conditions or crop types.

Straw or fodder balers, including pick-up balers, designed for collecting, compressing, and wrapping hay or fodder into bales. These machines are typically mounted on tractors and feature mechanisms for automatic pickup, baling, and wrapping.

Combine harvester-threshers designed for harvesting, threshing, and cleaning grain crops. These machines feature cutting heads, threshing mechanisms, and cleaning systems for efficient grain collection and separation.

Other threshing machinery not covered in preceding categories, including specialized equipment for grain or seed processing. These machines may vary in design and application depending on crop type or field conditions.

Potato diggers and potato harvesters designed for harvesting potatoes from the soil. These machines feature digging blades, conveyors, and sorting mechanisms for efficient potato collection and processing.

Machines designed specifically for harvesting sugar beets, including those that cut, lift, and transport beets from the field. These machines typically operate in agricultural settings and are built for heavy-duty use in root crop harvesting. They may include beet-top removal and root extraction components.

Miscellaneous beet harvesting or processing equipment not classified elsewhere. These may include small-scale or specialized machines for beet handling, such as transport conveyors, cleaning units, or auxiliary support devices used in conjunction with primary harvesters.

Self-propelled beet harvesting machines with their own motive power. These are mobile units capable of operating independently in fields, often equipped with wheels or tracks for maneuverability and designed for efficient beet collection without external towing.

Beet harvesting or processing machines not self-propelled or otherwise classified. This includes manually operated, towed, or semi-automated equipment used for beet collection, such as trailer-mounted harvesters or auxiliary sorting devices.

Other beet harvesting or processing machinery not specified elsewhere, including specialized tools for beet cleaning, grading, or storage. These may be stationary or mobile units designed for post-harvest handling of beets.

Machines used for cleaning, sorting, or grading eggs, fruits, vegetables, or other agricultural products. These may include conveyor belts, optical sorters, or automated grading systems designed for agricultural produce handling in processing plants or packing facilities.

Parts and accessories for beet harvesting or processing machinery. This includes components such as blades, conveyors, sensors, motors, or structural parts used in maintenance or repair of beet harvesting equipment.

Machines designed for milking cows or other livestock. These typically include automated or semi-automated systems with vacuum pumps, teat cups, and milk collection units, often used in dairy farms to extract milk efficiently and hygienically.

Machinery for dairy processing, including equipment for milk pasteurization, homogenization, and packaging. These machines handle milk in bulk, often in industrial dairy facilities, to ensure product safety and consistency.

Parts and accessories for milking or dairy processing machines. This includes pumps, valves, sensors, nozzles, and mechanical components used in dairy operations or required for maintenance or repair of dairy machinery.

General-purpose machinery used for various agricultural or food processing applications. These may include power units, conveyors, or control systems for farm equipment, not classified elsewhere under agricultural machinery.

Parts and accessories for general-purpose machinery used in agriculture or food processing. This includes mechanical, electrical, or structural components required for repair, maintenance, or replacement of machinery not specified elsewhere.

Machinery for preparing animal feed, including grinding, mixing, pelleting, or extruding equipment. These machines process grains, proteins, and additives into feed suitable for livestock, often used in feed manufacturing plants or large-scale farms.

Incubators and brooders for poultry, designed to maintain optimal temperature and humidity for egg hatching and chick rearing. These machines are typically used in poultry farms or hatcheries to ensure controlled breeding environments.

Other machinery used in poultry or animal husbandry, including ventilation systems, feeding equipment, or environmental control units not classified elsewhere. These may be auxiliary devices supporting animal care or housing.

Machinery specifically designed for forestry operations, including tree planting machines, wood harvesting equipment, or log transport systems. These are used in forest management, timber production, or reforestation projects.

Other forestry or agricultural machinery not specified elsewhere, including specialized tools for land clearing, soil preparation, or tree maintenance. These may be used in mixed-use agricultural or forestry settings.

Machinery for poultry keeping or poultry incubation, including feeders, waterers, or climate control units. These are designed to support poultry operations and ensure optimal conditions for animal health and productivity.

Other machinery not classified elsewhere, including auxiliary equipment for animal husbandry, poultry, or agricultural processing. These may be small-scale tools, support systems, or specialized devices for farm use.

Machinery for cleaning, sorting, or grading agricultural seeds, grains, or dried leguminous vegetables. These machines may include sieves, air classifiers, or optical sorters used in agricultural processing facilities.

Other machinery for agricultural or food processing applications, including equipment for crop handling, drying, or packaging. These may be specialized tools used in processing facilities or farms not covered elsewhere.

Parts and accessories for agricultural or food processing machinery. This includes mechanical, electrical, or structural components used for maintenance, repair, or replacement of machinery not specified elsewhere.

Machinery for baking, including mixers, kneaders, ovens, or conveyors used in bread or pastry production. These are typically found in commercial bakeries or food processing facilities for large-scale production.

Machinery for manufacturing macaroni, spaghetti, or similar pasta products, including extrusion, drying, or cutting equipment. These machines are used in pasta production facilities for consistent, large-scale pasta manufacturing.

Machinery for producing confectionery, cocoa, or chocolate, including mixing, tempering, molding, or coating equipment. These are used in confectionery plants to manufacture chocolate bars, candies, or cocoa-based products.

Machinery for sugar production, including evaporators, crystallizers, or centrifuges used in sugar refineries. These machines process raw sugar cane or beet juice into refined sugar products for commercial use.

Brewery machinery for beer or other beverage production, including fermentation tanks, bottling lines, or pasteurization equipment. These are used in breweries to manufacture and package alcoholic beverages.

Machinery for preparing meat or poultry, including cutting, deboning, chilling, or packaging equipment. These are used in meat processing plants to handle, process, and package meat products for distribution.

Machinery for preparing fruits, nuts, or vegetables, including washing, peeling, slicing, or packaging equipment. These are used in food processing plants to prepare and package fresh or processed produce.

Machinery for preparing coffee or tea, including roasters, grinders, or brewing units. These are used in coffee or tea processing facilities or commercial establishments for coffee or tea preparation.

Machinery for preparing or manufacturing beverages, including mixing, bottling, or carbonation equipment. These are used in beverage production facilities for producing soft drinks, juices, or other drink products.
Other machinery not specified elsewhere, including auxiliary equipment for beverage or food processing. These may be specialized tools or support devices used in beverage or food manufacturing.

Parts and accessories for beverage or food processing machinery. This includes mechanical, electrical, or structural components used for maintenance, repair, or replacement of machinery not specified elsewhere.

Machinery for producing pulp from fibrous cellulosic materials, including pulping, screening, or washing equipment. These are used in pulp mills to process wood or other raw materials into pulp for paper production.

Machinery for producing paper or paperboard, including paper machines, dryers, or calenders. These are used in paper mills to convert pulp into paper or paperboard for packaging, printing, or other uses.

Machinery for finishing paper or paperboard, including coating, laminating, or cutting equipment. These are used in paper finishing plants to enhance or prepare paper for final use in packaging or printing.
Parts and accessories for machinery used in pulp production. This includes rollers, blades, nozzles, or sensors used in pulping or pulp processing equipment for maintenance or repair.

Other machinery not specified elsewhere, including auxiliary equipment for paper or pulp processing. These may be specialized tools or support devices used in paper mills or pulp processing facilities.

Folding machines used in bookbinding or printing, including automatic or manual folding units for paper or cardstock. These are used in publishing or printing facilities to fold printed materials into book formats.

Collating and gathering machines used in bookbinding or printing, including devices for assembling pages into booklets or bound volumes. These are used in publishing or printing facilities to prepare printed materials for binding.

Sewing, wire stitching and stapling machines designed for binding books or documents. These machines use needles, staples, or wires to join materials. Typically used in publishing or office environments, they handle paper, fabric, or synthetic materials. Machines may be manual or automated, with varying speeds and capacities for different binding needs.

Unsewn (perfect) binding machines that bind documents using adhesive or glue without stitching. These machines typically apply adhesive to the spine or edges of pages and press them together under heat or pressure. Used in bookbinding and document production, they handle paper or cardstock, and may include features for trimming, gluing, or finishing.

Other bookbinding machines not classified elsewhere, including specialized equipment for binding, folding, or finishing documents. These may include manual or semi-automatic devices for gluing, stitching, or attaching covers. Typically used in small-scale or custom binding operations, they handle paper, cardstock, or synthetic materials.

Parts for bookbinding machines, including mechanical components, motors, gears, or control systems. These parts are not complete machines but are necessary for the operation or assembly of bookbinding equipment. May include blades, rollers, spindles, or electronic controllers used in sewing, stapling, or gluing machines.

Combined reel slitting and re-reeling machines that cut large paper or film reels into smaller widths and rewind them onto new spools. Used in printing, packaging, or film production, these machines handle continuous rolls of paper, plastic, or film. They feature precision cutting blades and automated rewinding mechanisms.

Slitting and cross-cutting machines that cut continuous rolls or sheets into narrower strips or individual pieces. Slitting machines cut along the length, while cross-cutting machines cut across the width. Used in packaging, printing, or industrial cutting, they handle paper, film, or plastic materials with precision blades or rotary cutters.

Guillotine-style cutting machines that cut sheets or rolls of material into straight lines using a heavy, descending blade. Used in printing, packaging, or manufacturing, they handle paper, cardboard, plastic, or metal. Machines may be manual or automated, with adjustable blade heights and safety guards.

Other cutting machines not classified elsewhere, including rotary cutters, laser cutters, or waterjet cutters. These machines cut materials like paper, plastic, metal, or fabric using mechanical, thermal, or fluid-based processes. May be used in industrial, commercial, or craft applications with varying precision and speed.

Machines for manufacturing bags, sacks, or envelopes from paper, plastic, or textile materials. These machines may include forming, sealing, or folding mechanisms. Used in packaging, agriculture, or postal industries, they handle continuous rolls or pre-cut sheets of material to produce flexible containers.

Machines for producing cartons, boxes, cases, tubes, drums, or similar containers without using molds. These machines may use folding, gluing, or assembly techniques. Used in packaging, logistics, or industrial production, they handle paperboard, plastic, or metal materials to form rigid or semi-rigid containers.

Machines for molding articles from paper pulp, paper, or paperboard using wet or dry processes. These machines may include presses, dryers, or forming rollers. Used in manufacturing paper products like wallpaper, pulpboard, or packaging materials, they handle wet pulp or dry paper stock to form rigid or flexible shapes.

Other machinery not classified elsewhere, including specialized equipment for cutting, forming, or processing materials. These may include manual or automated devices for handling paper, plastic, or metal. Used in industrial, commercial, or craft applications, they may be adapted for specific production needs.

Parts for cutting machines, including blades, rollers, spindles, or control systems. These parts are not complete machines but are essential for the operation or assembly of cutting equipment. May include cutting blades, guide rails, or motor assemblies used in guillotine, slitting, or rotary cutters.

Other parts or accessories for machinery not classified elsewhere, including bearings, gears, motors, or control components. These parts support the operation of various machines and may be used in cutting, forming, or processing equipment. May be interchangeable or specific to particular models.

Machinery, apparatus, and equipment for graphic arts, including presses, cutters, or finishing devices. These machines handle paper, film, or plastic materials for printing, cutting, or assembly. Used in publishing, advertising, or packaging, they may be manual or automated with varying capacities.

Parts for the machinery, apparatus, or equipment mentioned in heading 84423000. These include mechanical components, motors, gears, or control systems. Parts may be interchangeable or specific to particular machines, supporting the operation of graphic arts equipment.

Printing plates, cylinders, and lithographic stones prepared for graphic use. These may be planed, polished, or grained for improved ink transfer. Used in offset, letterpress, or lithographic printing, they handle ink and paper or other substrates for high-quality print reproduction.

Offset printing machines fed with paper from reels. These machines use ink rollers and plates to transfer ink onto continuous paper rolls. Used in high-volume printing, they handle paper, plastic, or film materials and are typically installed in commercial or industrial printing facilities.

Offset printing machines fed with sheets, designed for office use. These machines handle sheets with maximum dimensions of 22 cm by 36 cm in unfolded state. Used in small offices or commercial print shops, they are typically manual or semi-automated for low to medium print volumes.

Used printing machines, including offset, letterpress, or flexographic types. These machines may have been previously used in commercial, industrial, or office settings. They may require maintenance or refurbishment before reuse, but retain functional capacity for printing.

Offset printing machines with maximum sheet size not exceeding 53 cm by 75 cm. These machines are designed for smaller print jobs or office use. They handle paper, plastic, or film materials and are typically used in commercial or small-scale printing operations.

Offset printing machines with sheet size exceeding 53 cm by 75 cm but not exceeding 75 cm by 107 cm. These machines handle larger print jobs and are used in commercial or industrial printing. They may include automatic feeding, ink rollers, or plate systems for high-volume production.

Offset printing machines with sheet size exceeding 75 cm by 107 cm. These machines handle very large print jobs and are used in industrial or commercial printing. They may include high-capacity ink systems, automatic feeding, or specialized plate handling for large-format printing.

Other offset printing machines not classified elsewhere, including machines for special applications or sizes. These may include machines for textile printing, packaging, or large-format printing. They handle paper, plastic, or film materials and may require specific maintenance or adjustments.

Letterpress printing machines fed with paper from reels, excluding flexographic printing. These machines use engraved plates and ink rollers to transfer ink onto paper. Used in commercial or industrial printing, they handle paper or other substrates and are typically installed in printing facilities.

Letterpress printing machines fed with paper from sheets, excluding flexographic printing. These machines use engraved plates and ink rollers to transfer ink onto paper. Used in commercial or industrial printing, they handle paper or other substrates and are typically installed in printing facilities.

Flexographic printing machines that use flexible relief plates to transfer ink onto substrates. These machines handle paper, plastic, or film materials and are used in commercial, industrial, or packaging printing. They may include automatic feeding, ink rollers, or drying systems.

Gravure printing machines that use engraved cylinders to transfer ink onto substrates. These machines handle paper, plastic, or film materials and are used in commercial, industrial, or packaging printing. They may include automatic feeding, ink rollers, or drying systems.

Printing machines designed specifically for printing on textile materials. These machines handle fabrics, textiles, or other flexible substrates and may include specialized ink systems, drying mechanisms, or heat presses. Used in textile printing or garment production.

Printing machines designed for use in semiconductor manufacturing. These machines handle specialized substrates like silicon wafers and may include precision ink systems, UV curing, or cleanroom-compatible components. Used in electronics or microchip production.

Other printing machines not classified elsewhere, including machines for special applications or substrates. These may include machines for packaging, textiles, or industrial printing. They handle paper, plastic, or film materials and may require specific maintenance or adjustments.

Machines capable of performing two or more functions: printing, copying, or facsimile transmission. These machines may connect to automatic data-processing systems or networks. Used in office, commercial, or industrial environments, they handle paper or digital media for multifunctional output.

Printers that produce printed output from digital or analog sources. These machines handle paper, plastic, or film materials and may include inkjet, laser, or dot-matrix technologies. Used in commercial, office, or industrial printing, they may be standalone or networked.

Other printing machines not classified elsewhere, including specialized or manual devices. These machines may handle paper, plastic, or film materials and may be used in commercial, industrial, or craft applications. May include manual or automated devices for printing.

Other machines and apparatus for printing, including specialized or manual devices. These machines may handle paper, plastic, or film materials and may be used in commercial, industrial, or craft applications. May include manual or automated devices for printing.

Parts for machines designed for semiconductor manufacturing, including components for ink systems, UV curing, or cleanroom compatibility. These parts support the operation of specialized printing machines used in electronics or microchip production.

Printing machines made of cast iron or cast steel, including components for ink rollers, plate systems, or feeding mechanisms. These machines are durable and used in commercial or industrial printing applications, handling paper or other substrates.

Other parts or accessories for printing machines not classified elsewhere, including mechanical, electronic, or control components. These parts support the operation of various printing machines and may be interchangeable or specific to particular models.

Electronic assemblies for printing machines, including circuit boards, integrated circuits, or control modules. These assemblies support the operation of printing machines and may be used in digital or analog printing systems.

Other printing-related items not classified elsewhere, including accessories, spare parts, or consumables. These items may be used in maintenance, repair, or operation of printing machines and may include ink, rollers, or cleaning agents.

Machines designed for extrusion processes, typically used to force materials like plastics, metals, or rubber through a die to form continuous shapes such as pipes, profiles, or sheets. These machines often include screw extruders, die-cutting systems, and cooling units for controlled output.

Miscellaneous machinery not classified elsewhere in Chapter 84, including auxiliary units, testing equipment, or specialized tools used in textile or plastics manufacturing, but not covered under specific extrusion or spinning categories.

Carding machines that prepare raw fibers—such as cotton, wool, or synthetic fibers—by combing and aligning them to remove impurities and create a uniform web. These machines are essential in textile production before spinning.

Combing machines that further refine carded fibers by removing shorter or irregular fibers, ensuring only longer, cleaner fibers are processed for higher-quality yarns. These machines often use combing rollers and precision blades.

Drawing or roving machines that stretch and twist fibers to form continuous strands called roving, preparing them for spinning. These machines control tension and fiber alignment to ensure consistent yarn quality.
Other textile fiber processing machines not covered elsewhere, including those for fiber blending, cleaning, or preparation for spinning, but not specifically carding, combing, or drawing machines.

Spinning machines that convert prepared fibers into continuous yarns by twisting and drawing them under controlled tension. These machines may include ring spinning, rotor spinning, or open-end spinning systems.
Textile doubling or twisting machines that combine two or more single yarns into a stronger, multi-ply yarn. These machines control twist levels and ply alignment for fabric strength and texture.

Textile winding or reeling machines that wind yarn onto spools or bobbins. These include weft-winding machines for weaving and reeling machines for fiber preparation, often with automatic tension control and spool change mechanisms.
Other textile machinery not covered under spinning, winding, or carding, including machines for yarn dyeing, finishing, or testing, used in textile production for quality control or treatment.

Looms designed for weaving fabrics with a width not exceeding 30 cm, typically used for narrow textiles such as trimmings, ribbons, or small-scale fabric production. These machines are often manual or semi-automatic.

Power-driven weaving looms that automate the weaving process using motors and programmable controls. These machines are used for high-volume, consistent fabric production, including cotton, silk, and synthetic fabrics.
Other weaving looms not specified elsewhere, including hand-operated or semi-automatic looms for weaving fabrics wider than 30 cm, used for specialty textiles or small-batch production.

Shuttleless weaving machines designed for weaving fabrics wider than 30 cm without shuttle mechanisms. These machines use rapier, airjet, or waterjet technology for high-speed, wide-woven fabric production.

Warp knitting machines with cylinder diameters not exceeding 165 mm, used for producing knitted fabrics with warp threads. These machines are suitable for lightweight, stretchable textiles like underwear or swimwear.

Warp knitting machines with cylinder diameters exceeding 165 mm, suitable for larger-scale production of knitted fabrics. These machines are used for heavy-duty textiles, industrial fabrics, or garments requiring high output.

Warp knitting machines including Raschel type and stitch-bonding machines, used for producing textured, stretchable, or bonded knitted fabrics. These machines are often used in fashion, home textiles, and technical fabrics.
Other warp knitting machines not specified elsewhere, including specialized knitting machines for producing unique fabric structures or for industrial applications such as conveyor belts or filters.

Other knitting machines not classified elsewhere, including specialized machines for producing non-woven knitted fabrics or for specific applications like medical textiles, automotive textiles, or technical fabrics.

Dobby and jacquard machines used for creating intricate patterns in woven fabrics. These machines also include card-reducing, copying, punching, or assembling machines for preparing and modifying jacquard cards.
Other machinery not specified elsewhere, including auxiliary devices or tools for pattern design, fabric inspection, or pattern transfer used in woven textile production.

Parts and accessories for machines under heading 8444 or their auxiliary machinery, including spare parts, tools, or components necessary for maintenance or operation of extrusion or textile machinery.
Card clothing, including metal or wire combs used in carding machines to comb and align fibers. These are consumable parts that require regular replacement to maintain fiber quality.
Other accessories for textile fiber preparation machines, excluding card clothing, such as cleaning brushes, fiber guides, or feed rollers used to handle and process raw fibers.

Spindles, spindle flyers, spinning rings, and ring travelers used in spinning machines to maintain tension and guide fibers during yarn production. These components are critical for consistent yarn quality.
Other parts and accessories for textile machinery not specified elsewhere, including bearings, gears, or lubrication systems used to maintain operation of spinning or winding equipment.
Reeds for looms, healds, and heald-frames used to control warp thread movement during weaving. These components ensure correct thread positioning and pattern formation in woven fabrics.
Other accessories for weaving machines not specified elsewhere, including shuttle components, thread guides, or tensioning devices used to support weaving operations.
Sinkers, also known as platins, used in looms to control the position of warp threads during weaving. These components are typically made of metal and are designed for specific thread densities.
Other loom accessories not specified elsewhere, including thread guides, tension regulators, or shuttle components used to support weaving operations and ensure consistent fabric production.
Other accessories for textile machinery not specified elsewhere, including tools, gauges, or cleaning equipment used for maintenance or operation of textile production machines.

Machinery for manufacturing or finishing felt or nonwovens, including machines for cutting, bonding, or shaping nonwoven fabrics. These also include hat-making blocks for producing felt hats or other shaped articles.
Front-loading washing machines designed for household or commercial use, with a front-opening door for loading laundry. These machines typically feature automatic wash, rinse, and spin cycles.
Top-loading washing machines with a top-opening lid for loading laundry. These machines often feature automatic wash, rinse, and spin cycles, with built-in agitators or impellers for cleaning.

Washing machines with a dry linen capacity exceeding 6 kg but not exceeding 10 kg, designed for household or small commercial use. These machines typically include automatic wash, rinse, and spin cycles.
Washing machines with built-in centrifugal dryers, designed for automatic drying after washing. These machines include drum rotation and heat exchange mechanisms for efficient drying.
Other washing machines not specified elsewhere, including semi-automatic or manual washing machines for household or commercial use, with or without built-in dryers.
Washing machines with a dry linen capacity exceeding 10 kg, designed for commercial or heavy-duty use. These machines are typically larger and feature robust motors and automatic cycles.
Parts and accessories for washing machines, including motors, drums, washers, filters, or control panels used to maintain or repair washing machines.
Dry-cleaning machines designed for cleaning textiles using chemical solvents. These machines include solvent recovery systems, heating units, and filtration systems for safe and efficient cleaning.

Dry linen pressing machines with a capacity not exceeding 10 kg, designed for pressing dry textiles such as cotton or linen, typically used in laundries or commercial laundry settings for efficient, high-volume pressing operations.

Pressing machines not classified elsewhere, including specialized models for pressing textiles or garments with capacities exceeding 10 kg, often used in industrial or commercial laundries for heavy-duty pressing tasks.

Ironing and pressing machines, including fusing presses used to bond fabrics or materials, typically found in garment manufacturing or textile finishing, designed for applying heat and pressure to smooth or fuse materials.

Washing, bleaching, and dyeing machines designed for textile processing, capable of handling large volumes of fabric, typically used in textile mills or dye houses for chemical treatment and color application.

Machines for reeling, unreeling, folding, cutting, or pinking textile fabrics, typically used in textile manufacturing for preparing fabric rolls or trimming edges, often featuring automated cutting mechanisms.

Machines for applying adhesive paste to textile fabrics or other support materials during the production of linoleum or similar floor coverings, typically featuring rollers or spreaders for even paste distribution.

Machines for dressing or finishing textile products, including those used for surface treatments such as coating, embossing, or calendering, typically found in textile finishing lines for enhancing fabric appearance or durability.

Other machinery not classified elsewhere in this heading, including auxiliary machines for textile processing such as conveyors, tensioners, or material handling devices used in textile manufacturing.
Parts and components of machines used for textile processing, including rollers, gears, motors, or control systems, typically used to maintain or repair textile machinery for continuous operation.

Sewing machines valued over €65 (excluding frames, tables, or furniture), typically used in garment manufacturing or upholstery, designed for high-speed stitching with automatic thread tension and needle control.

Other sewing machines not classified elsewhere, including hand-operated or semi-automatic models used in small-scale or custom sewing applications, often designed for precision stitching in textiles or leather.

Other sewing machines and sewing machine heads, including industrial or domestic models with interchangeable needles or presser feet, typically used for stitching fabrics, leather, or other flexible materials.

Automatic sewing machines capable of performing multiple operations without manual intervention, including thread feeding, needle positioning, and stitch pattern control, typically used in high-volume garment production.
Other sewing machines not classified elsewhere, including semi-automatic or manually operated machines used in small-scale or specialized sewing applications such as quilting or embroidery.
Sewing machine needles designed for use in various sewing machines, typically made of hardened steel, used for piercing and stitching fabric or other materials with precision and durability.
Furniture, bases, and covers for sewing machines, along with other parts such as spools, thread guides, or feed mechanisms, typically used to support or enhance the functionality of sewing machines.

Machinery for preparing, tanning, or working hides, skins, or leather, including drum tanneries, chemical treatment units, or drying systems, typically used in leather processing facilities for converting raw hides into finished leather.

Machinery for making or repairing footwear, including cutting machines, stitching units, or sole-attaching equipment, typically used in footwear manufacturing or repair shops for assembling or repairing shoes.
Other machinery not classified elsewhere in this heading, including auxiliary equipment such as conveyors, cleaning systems, or material handling devices used in leather or footwear processing.
Parts and components of machinery used in leather or footwear processing, including rollers, blades, or motors, typically used to maintain or repair machinery for continuous operation.
Converters used in metal casting, designed to change the shape or form of molten metal, typically used in foundries for producing ingots or semi-finished metal products with controlled dimensions.
Ingot moulds and ladles used in metal casting, including moulds for shaping metal ingots and ladles for pouring molten metal, typically used in foundries for producing standardized metal shapes.

Machines for casting metal under pressure, including hydraulic or mechanical systems for injecting molten metal into molds, typically used in high-precision metal casting for components requiring tight tolerances.

Other metal casting machines not classified elsewhere, including machines for sand casting, die casting, or investment casting, typically used in foundries for producing metal parts with varying complexity.
Parts and components of metal casting machines, including nozzles, pistons, or mold release systems, typically used to maintain or repair casting equipment for continuous production.
Tube mills used for rolling or shaping metal tubes, typically used in metal fabrication for producing seamless or welded tubes with precise dimensions and wall thickness.

Rolling mills designed for hot or combined hot and cold rolling, including rolling stands with heating systems or dual-temperature rollers, typically used in steel mills for shaping metal sheets or strips.

Rolling mills designed exclusively for cold rolling, including cold-rolling stands with cooling systems or lubrication mechanisms, typically used in metal fabrication for producing thin sheets with high surface quality.
Rolling mills made of cast iron, typically used in heavy-duty metal rolling operations, designed for durability and heat resistance in industrial metal processing environments.

Hot-rolling work rolls and hot-rolling and cold-rolling back-up rolls used in metal rolling mills, typically designed for high-temperature applications and supporting the rolling process for metal sheets or strips.

Cold-rolling work rolls used in metal rolling mills, typically designed for precision cold rolling of metal sheets or strips, often featuring hardened surfaces for durability and dimensional accuracy.
Other rolling mill components not classified elsewhere, including auxiliary rollers, bearings, or drive systems used in metal rolling operations for supporting or guiding the rolling process.
Other parts of rolling mills, including bearings, gears, or control mechanisms, typically used to maintain or repair rolling equipment for continuous metal shaping operations.

Laser machines used exclusively or principally for manufacturing printed circuits, printed circuit assemblies, or parts of automatic data processing machines, typically featuring precision laser cutting or etching capabilities.
Other laser machines not classified elsewhere, including general-purpose laser cutting or engraving machines used in industrial or commercial applications for metal, plastic, or wood.

Laser machines used exclusively or principally for manufacturing printed circuits, printed circuit assemblies, or parts of automatic data processing machines, typically featuring precision laser cutting or etching capabilities.
Other laser machines not classified elsewhere, including general-purpose laser cutting or engraving machines used in industrial or commercial applications for metal, plastic, or wood.

Machines operated by ultrasonic processes, including ultrasonic welding, cleaning, or cutting equipment, typically used in industrial applications for joining or treating materials with high precision.

Wire-cut machines using wire electrodes to cut materials, typically used in metalworking for precision cutting of hard materials such as steel or titanium, often used in aerospace or industrial manufacturing.
Other machines not classified elsewhere, including general-purpose cutting or shaping machines used in industrial or commercial applications for metal, plastic, or wood.

Lasertool machines, other than those specified elsewhere, designed for precision cutting or drilling using laser energy, typically used in metalworking or fabrication industries, often equipped with automated controls and programmable settings for high-accuracy material processing.

Machines that utilize plasma arc processes for cutting, drilling, or shaping metallic materials, typically featuring high-temperature plasma jets generated by electrical discharges, used in heavy industrial applications such as shipbuilding or steel fabrication.

Water-jet cutting machines that use high-pressure water streams, often mixed with abrasive particles, to cut through metals, ceramics, or composites with precision; commonly used in manufacturing for intricate or non-conductive material processing.

Other laser, plasma, or water-jet cutting machines not covered elsewhere, including hybrid or multi-functional systems, typically used for precision material processing in industries like aerospace, automotive, or electronics manufacturing.

Horizontal boring and milling machines designed for large-scale, multi-axis machining of heavy workpieces, typically mounted on a stable base, used for producing large structural components in shipbuilding or heavy machinery industries.

Other boring and milling machines not specified elsewhere, including multi-functional units capable of drilling, boring, or milling operations, typically used in heavy industry for processing large or complex metal parts.

Unit construction machines with single stations, designed for sequential machining operations, typically used in assembly lines or batch production of components requiring multiple machining steps without repositioning.

Numerically controlled boring and milling machines with programmable motion control, capable of performing complex machining operations with high precision, commonly used in aerospace, automotive, or precision tool manufacturing.

Other boring and milling machines not specified elsewhere, including manually operated or semi-automated units, typically used for general-purpose machining of metal components in industrial or workshop settings.

Turning centres with automatic tool changers and multi-axis control, capable of performing turning, boring, and drilling operations on cylindrical workpieces, commonly used in high-volume production of shafts or components.

Single-spindle turning machines designed for turning, facing, and drilling operations on cylindrical workpieces, typically used for producing shafts, rods, or components requiring high-precision cylindrical features.

Multi-spindle turning machines with synchronized spindles, capable of processing multiple workpieces simultaneously, typically used for high-volume production of small, complex components like bearings or fasteners.
Other turning machines not specified elsewhere, including manually operated or semi-automated units, typically used for general-purpose turning operations in workshops or small-scale production.
Other turning machines not covered elsewhere, including manual or semi-automated units for turning operations, typically used in small-scale or custom metalworking applications.

Aluminum alloy products used in building construction, including structural members, roofing panels, or cladding materials. These are typically extruded or rolled and designed for durability, corrosion resistance, and load-bearing applications in architectural frameworks.

Miscellaneous aluminum alloy products not covered elsewhere, including decorative or non-structural aluminum components used in architectural or industrial applications. These may include profiles, sheets, or tubes used for aesthetic or functional purposes.

Aluminum alloy products incorporating light-emitting diodes (LEDs) for illumination purposes. These may include integrated lighting fixtures, display panels, or signage components designed for energy-efficient, long-lasting illumination in architectural or commercial applications.

Aluminum alloy products incorporating active matrix liquid crystal display (LCD) technology, used for electronic displays in signage, control panels, or visual communication systems. These components are typically thin, lightweight, and designed for high-resolution visual output.

Miscellaneous aluminum alloy products not covered elsewhere, including non-illuminated or non-display components used in architectural or industrial applications. These may include structural supports, decorative elements, or protective covers for aluminum-based systems.

Bells, buzzers, door chimes, and similar sound-emitting devices used for signaling or notification purposes. These are typically electrically powered, designed for integration into building automation, security systems, or communication devices, and produce audible alerts.

Miscellaneous electronic sound-emitting devices not covered elsewhere, including tone generators, siren modules, or signal indicators used for warning, communication, or control systems. These may be integrated into industrial, commercial, or residential applications for alerting purposes.

Parts and components for sound-emitting devices classified under heading 8531, including mechanical, electrical, or electronic parts used in the assembly or maintenance of signaling devices. These may include transducers, switches, or mounting hardware.

Fixed capacitors designed for use in 50/60 Hz AC power circuits with reactive power handling capacity of at least 0.5 kvar. These are typically used in power factor correction, motor starting, or power conditioning applications in industrial and commercial electrical systems.

Tantalum capacitors, which are electrolytic capacitors using tantalum as the anode material. These are used in applications requiring high reliability, compact size, and stable capacitance, such as in consumer electronics, automotive systems, or industrial control circuits.

Aluminum electrolytic capacitors, which use aluminum oxide as the dielectric. These are commonly used in power supply circuits, audio equipment, and industrial applications due to their high capacitance, durability, and cost-effectiveness.

Ceramic dielectric capacitors with a single layer of ceramic material, typically used in high-frequency applications. These are known for their small size, low cost, and stable performance, commonly found in consumer electronics, communication devices, and signal processing circuits.

Ceramic dielectric capacitors with multiple layers of ceramic material, designed for high capacitance and compact size. These are widely used in digital circuits, power supplies, and telecommunications equipment due to their reliability and performance at high frequencies.

Capacitors with dielectric material made of paper or plastics, typically used in older or specialized applications. These may include film capacitors used in audio equipment, industrial machinery, or legacy electronic systems.

Miscellaneous capacitors not covered elsewhere, including experimental, niche, or custom-designed capacitors used in specialized applications. These may include capacitors with non-standard materials, geometries, or performance characteristics.

Variable or adjustable (pre-set) capacitors designed for tuning or fine-tuning electrical circuits. These are commonly used in radio receivers, test equipment, or industrial control systems where precise capacitance adjustment is required.
Parts and components for capacitors classified under heading 8532, including terminals, leads, or mounting hardware. These are used in the assembly or repair of capacitor-based circuits and systems.

Fixed carbon resistors, including composition and film types, used for controlling current and voltage in electrical circuits. These are commonly found in analog circuits, power supplies, and signal conditioning applications due to their stability and cost-effectiveness.

Carbon resistors designed for power handling capacities not exceeding 20 W. These are typically used in low-power circuits, audio equipment, or consumer electronics where moderate power dissipation is required.

Miscellaneous carbon resistors not covered elsewhere, including high-precision or specialty resistors used in specialized applications. These may include resistors with non-standard tolerances, power ratings, or temperature coefficients.

Carbon resistors designed for power handling capacities not exceeding 20 W, similar to 85332100. These are used in low-power circuits, including audio equipment, consumer electronics, or test instruments.

Miscellaneous carbon resistors not covered elsewhere, including specialty or high-precision resistors used in specialized applications. These may include resistors with unique tolerances, temperature coefficients, or power ratings.

Carbon resistors designed for power handling capacities not exceeding 20 W, similar to 85332100 and 85333100. These are used in low-power circuits, including audio equipment, consumer electronics, or test instruments.

Miscellaneous carbon resistors not covered elsewhere, including specialty or high-precision resistors used in specialized applications. These may include resistors with unique tolerances, temperature coefficients, or power ratings.
Parts and components for resistors classified under heading 8533, including terminals, leads, or mounting hardware. These are used in the assembly or repair of resistor-based circuits and systems.

Multilayer circuits, which are printed circuit boards (PCBs) with multiple conductive layers. These are used in complex electronic devices requiring high-density interconnections, such as computers, telecommunications equipment, or industrial control systems.

Miscellaneous printed circuit boards (PCBs) not covered elsewhere, including single-layer, double-layer, or hybrid PCBs used in various electronic applications. These may include prototype boards, test fixtures, or specialized PCBs for niche applications.

Passive electrical components such as resistors, capacitors, and inductors, used in electronic circuits for impedance control, energy storage, or signal filtering. These components are not active devices and do not amplify or generate signals. Typically found in consumer electronics, industrial equipment, and communication systems.

Fuses designed to protect electrical circuits by breaking the circuit when excessive current flows. These are typically single-element devices with a metal wire or strip that melts to interrupt current flow. Commonly used in household appliances, automotive systems, and industrial machinery.

Circuit breakers designed for use in electrical systems operating at voltages below 72.5 kV. These are mechanical or electromechanical devices that interrupt current flow during overloads or short circuits. Used in low-voltage industrial and commercial installations.

Circuit breakers for applications not covered by other specific categories. These include specialized breakers for unique voltage or current requirements, custom configurations, or niche industrial applications not defined elsewhere in Chapter 85.

Fuses specifically designed for use in electrical systems operating at voltages below 72.5 kV. These are typically cartridge, plug, or blade fuses used in low-voltage applications such as consumer electronics, automotive systems, and industrial control panels.

Fuses for applications not covered by other specific categories. These include specialized fuses for unique current ratings, voltage levels, or environmental conditions not addressed elsewhere in Chapter 85.

Lightning arresters, voltage limiters, and surge suppressors designed to protect electrical equipment from transient overvoltages. These devices divert excess voltage to ground or limit voltage levels, often used in power distribution, telecommunications, and sensitive electronic systems.

Fuses and other protective devices not classified elsewhere in Chapter 85. This category includes miscellaneous protective components such as thermal cutouts, current-limiting devices, or specialized fuses for niche applications.

Fuses rated for current not exceeding 10 amperes. These are typically used in low-power circuits such as household appliances, automotive lighting, or small electronic devices requiring precise current limitation.

Fuses rated for currents exceeding 10 amperes but not exceeding 63 amperes. These are commonly used in industrial control panels, commercial appliances, and equipment requiring moderate current protection.

Fuses rated for currents exceeding 63 amperes. These are typically used in heavy-duty industrial equipment, large motors, or high-capacity power distribution systems requiring robust overcurrent protection.

Fuses rated for current not exceeding 63 amperes. These are designed for general-purpose use in circuits requiring moderate current protection, including commercial, industrial, and residential applications.
Fuses rated for currents exceeding 63 amperes. These are typically used in high-current circuits such as industrial machinery, power distribution systems, or large-scale electrical installations.

Fuses rated for current not exceeding 16 amperes. These are commonly used in residential and light commercial applications, including household appliances, lighting circuits, and small electronic devices.

Fuses rated for currents exceeding 16 amperes but not exceeding 125 amperes. These are typically used in industrial equipment, commercial appliances, or electrical systems requiring moderate to high current protection.
Fuses rated for currents exceeding 125 amperes. These are designed for heavy-duty applications such as industrial motors, large power distribution systems, or high-capacity electrical installations.
Fuses rated for current not exceeding 2 amperes. These are typically used in low-power circuits such as electronic devices, control panels, or applications requiring very precise current limitation.
Fuses rated for currents exceeding 2 amperes. These are used in applications requiring moderate current protection, such as consumer electronics, small appliances, or control circuits.

Fuses and other protective devices not classified elsewhere in Chapter 85. This includes miscellaneous protective components such as time-delay fuses, specialized thermal cutouts, or custom-designed protective devices.

Electronic AC switches consisting of optically coupled input and output circuits, specifically insulated thyristor AC switches. These are used in industrial and commercial applications for switching AC power with high isolation between control and load circuits.

Electronic switches, including temperature-protected types, composed of a transistor and a logic chip using chip-on-chip technology. These are used in modern electronics for controlled switching with thermal protection, such as in power supplies or automation systems.

Electromechanical snap-action switches rated for current not exceeding 11 amperes. These are used in control panels, lighting systems, and low-power applications where rapid, reliable switching is required.
Push-button switches designed for manual operation. These are commonly used in control panels, appliances, and machinery for initiating or stopping operations with a simple press.
Rotary switches designed for manual selection of multiple positions. These are used in appliances, control systems, and equipment where sequential or multi-position selection is required.

Other electronic or electromechanical switches not classified elsewhere. This includes specialized switches such as toggle, slide, or rotary switches for unique applications not covered by other categories.
Switches and other protective devices not classified elsewhere in Chapter 85. This includes miscellaneous switches such as momentary, latching, or custom-designed switches for niche applications.
Edison-style lamp holders designed for incandescent lamps. These are typically used in vintage or specialized lighting applications, including decorative fixtures or historical reproductions.
Other lamp holders not classified as Edison-type. These include holders for various lamp types such as halogen, fluorescent, or LED lamps, used in diverse lighting applications.
Connectors designed specifically for coaxial cables. These are used in telecommunications, radio, and video equipment for transmitting signals with minimal interference.
Connectors designed for printed circuit boards. These are used in electronics manufacturing for connecting components to PCBs, including pin headers, socket connectors, and surface-mount connectors.
Other connectors not classified elsewhere in Chapter 85. This includes specialized connectors for unique applications such as high-voltage, fiber-optic, or custom-designed connectors.

Connectors for optical fibers, including connectors for fiber bundles or cables. These are used in telecommunications, data transmission, and fiber-optic networking systems for high-speed data transfer.

Prefabricated elements designed for electrical circuits. These are pre-assembled components such as terminal blocks, connectors, or protective devices intended for easy integration into electrical systems.
Connection and contact elements designed for wires and cables. These include terminals, connectors, and junction boxes used to establish secure electrical connections between conductors.

Battery clamps specifically designed for use with motor vehicles classified under headings 8702, 8703, 8704, or 8711. These are used to connect batteries to vehicle electrical systems with secure, high-current connections.
Other electrical connection devices not classified elsewhere. This includes miscellaneous connectors, terminals, or contact elements for specialized applications not covered by other categories.

Numerical control panels equipped with built-in automatic data-processing machines. These are used in industrial machinery for programming and controlling automated processes based on numerical data input.

Programmable memory controllers designed to store and execute control programs. These are used in industrial automation, robotics, and programmable logic controllers (PLCs) for managing complex control sequences.

Touch-sensitive data input devices without display capabilities, designed for incorporation into display-equipped apparatus. These detect and locate touch inputs to enable user interaction with display systems without requiring a separate input device.

Other control or data input devices not classified elsewhere. This includes miscellaneous control interfaces, input devices, or specialized controllers for unique applications not covered by other categories.

High-voltage circuit breakers designed for electrical systems operating above 1000 volts but not exceeding 72.5 kilovolts. High-voltage circuit breakers rated for electrical systems exceeding 72.5 kilovolts. These are specialized equipment used in ultra-high-voltage transmission networks, substations, or heavy industrial applications requiring precise interruption of very high fault currents for grid stability and safety.

Unassembled bases such as boards, panels, consoles, desks, or cabinets intended for use with equipment under heading 8537. These are typically metal or plastic enclosures without internal electrical components, designed for mounting or housing electronic apparatus.

Pre-assembled electronic components combining multiple parts, such as resistors, capacitors, or integrated circuits, mounted on a single substrate. These are used in complex electronic systems and require no further assembly before installation into larger equipment.

Miscellaneous electronic goods not classified elsewhere under heading 8538. This includes small-scale electronic assemblies, connectors, or unclassified electronic accessories used in electrical or electronic apparatus.

Pre-assembled electronic components combining multiple parts, such as resistors, capacitors, or integrated circuits, mounted on a single substrate. These are used in complex electronic systems and require no further assembly before installation into larger equipment.

Miscellaneous electronic goods not classified elsewhere under heading 8538. This includes small-scale electronic assemblies, connectors, or unclassified electronic accessories used in electrical or electronic apparatus.

Sealed beam lamps, which are self-contained units combining a bulb, reflector, and lens in a single hermetically sealed housing. These are typically used in automotive or industrial lighting where durability and weather resistance are essential.

Lamps and tubes specifically designed for use in motorcycles or other motor vehicles. These include headlamps, taillights, or signal lamps, often built for durability, compact size, and vibration resistance in mobile applications.

Lamps and tubes with operating voltage exceeding 100 volts. These are typically used in automotive, industrial, or commercial lighting where higher voltage ensures efficient operation and reduced current draw in high-power systems.

Lamps and tubes with operating voltage not exceeding 100 volts. These are commonly used in low-voltage applications such as automotive interior lighting, portable equipment, or consumer electronics where safety and efficiency are critical.

Reflector lamps designed with a reflective surface to direct and focus light. These are commonly used in automotive headlamps, industrial lighting, or spotlights where beam control and intensity are essential.
Other lamps and tubes not classified elsewhere under heading 8539. This includes specialty lamps such as decorative, signaling, or industrial lamps with non-standard designs or applications.

Lamps and tubes specifically designed for use in motorcycles or other motor vehicles. These include headlamps, taillights, or signal lamps, often built for durability, compact size, and vibration resistance in mobile applications.

Lamps and tubes with operating voltage exceeding 100 volts. These are typically used in automotive, industrial, or commercial lighting where higher voltage ensures efficient operation and reduced current draw in high-power systems.

Lamps and tubes with operating voltage not exceeding 100 volts. These are commonly used in low-voltage applications such as automotive interior lighting, portable equipment, or consumer electronics where safety and efficiency are critical.

Lamps and tubes with double-ended caps, designed for secure electrical connection at both ends. These are often used in industrial or commercial lighting fixtures requiring reliable socket attachment and electrical safety.

Other lamps and tubes not classified elsewhere under heading 8539. This includes lamps with unconventional mounting, non-standard cap types, or specialized applications not covered by other subheadings.

Mercury or sodium vapor lamps that produce light by passing an electric current through vaporized metal. These are used in high-intensity lighting applications such as street lighting, industrial lighting, or large-scale illumination systems.

Metal halide lamps that generate light by exciting metal halide vapor. These lamps are used in high-brightness applications such as stadium lighting, film studios, or commercial lighting where color rendering and efficiency are critical.

Cold-cathode fluorescent lamps (CCFLs) specifically designed for backlighting flat-panel displays. These lamps are compact, low-power, and used in LCD screens for uniform illumination behind the display panel.

Arc lamps that produce light by creating an electric arc between two electrodes. These are used in high-intensity applications such as searchlights, projection systems, or industrial lighting where bright, continuous illumination is required.

Other ultraviolet lamps, including those designed for sterilization, curing, or inspection applications. These lamps emit UV radiation and are used in laboratories, medical facilities, or manufacturing processes requiring UV light.

Light-emitting diode (LED) modules, which are compact, energy-efficient light sources composed of multiple diodes mounted on a circuit board. These are used in lighting systems, displays, or signaling devices where energy efficiency is critical.

Lamp bases designed for mounting and connecting lamps to electrical circuits or fixtures. These bases are typically made of metal or plastic and include electrical contacts for secure lamp attachment and power delivery.

Color cathode ray tubes designed for use in color television sets or monitors. These tubes produce color images by using phosphor screens excited by electron beams, typically with red, green, and blue color filters.

Monochrome cathode ray tubes designed for use in black-and-white television sets or monitors. These tubes produce images using a single phosphor screen excited by electron beams, commonly used in older television and display technologies.

Television camera tubes designed to capture and convert optical images into electrical signals. These tubes are used in analog television cameras for broadcasting or recording video, typically with image sensors based on electron beams.

Data or graphic display tubes for monochrome or color display. Monochrome tubes use phosphor screens, while color tubes feature phosphor dot screens with a pitch smaller than 0.4 mm, enabling high-resolution graphic display for computer monitors or terminals.
Other cathode ray tubes not classified elsewhere under heading 8540. This includes specialized tubes for radar, medical imaging, or industrial inspection systems requiring electron beam scanning.

Magnetrons designed to generate microwave radiation. These tubes are used in microwave ovens, radar systems, or industrial heating equipment where microwave energy is required for heating or signal transmission.
Other cathode ray tubes not classified elsewhere under heading 8540. This includes specialized tubes for radar, medical imaging, or industrial inspection systems requiring electron beam scanning.

Receiver or amplifier valves and tubes designed to amplify or receive radio frequency signals. These are used in radio receivers, television tuners, or communication equipment requiring signal amplification or frequency conversion.
Other cathode ray tubes not classified elsewhere under heading 8540. This includes specialized tubes for radar, medical imaging, or industrial inspection systems requiring electron beam scanning.

Cathode ray tubes specifically designed for use with other cathode ray tubes or related equipment. These are typically used in systems requiring signal processing or display synchronization with other CRTs or components.

Light-emitting diodes (LEDs) made from semiconductor materials such as gallium arsenide or indium phosphide, emitting visible light when current passes through, used in lighting, displays, and indicator applications.

Artillery weapons including cannons, howitzers, and mortars. These are heavy-caliber firearms designed for firing explosive shells over long distances. Typically made of steel or alloy, they are used by military forces for direct or indirect fire support. They may be mounted on wheeled carriages or static emplacements.

Rocket launchers, flame-throwers, grenade launchers, torpedo tubes, and similar projectile-discharging devices. These include portable or vehicle-mounted systems designed for launching explosive or incendiary munitions. Materials vary but often include metal, composite, and polymer components. Used in military or tactical operations for area denial or close combat.

Other military weapons not specified elsewhere in Chapter 93. This includes miscellaneous firearms, anti-tank weapons, or experimental systems not covered under other headings. Typically constructed from durable metals and designed for specialized combat roles. May include prototypes, experimental models, or niche tactical equipment.

Revolvers and pistols excluding those classified under 9303 or 9304. These are typically single-action or double-action firearms with revolving cylinders. Materials include steel, aluminum, or polymer. Used for personal defense, law enforcement, or military support. Often chambered for small-caliber ammunition.

Muzzle-loading firearms, including flintlock, percussion, or caplock systems. These require loading powder and projectile through the muzzle. Typically made of iron or steel with wooden or metal stocks. Used historically or in ceremonial contexts. May include antique or replica models for collectors or museums.

Single-barrelled smoothbore firearms, typically muzzle-loading or early breech-loading models. These have ungrooved barrels and are often used for hunting or historical reenactments. Materials include iron, steel, or brass. Caliber ranges from small to medium. Often feature wooden stocks and metal fittings.

Other muzzle-loading firearms not specified elsewhere. Includes rare or obsolete models such as matchlock, wheellock, or early percussion firearms. Typically made of iron or steel with wooden or leather components. Used for historical display, restoration, or specialized applications such as marksmanship training or museum exhibits.

Other sporting, hunting, or target-shooting rifles. These are precision firearms designed for accuracy and control. Materials include steel, aluminum, or composite. Often feature detachable magazines, adjustable stocks, and rifled barrels. Used for hunting game, competitive shooting, or recreational target practice.

Other firearms including signal pistols or specialized models not covered elsewhere. These may include flashbang launchers, flare guns, or signaling devices. Materials vary but often include metal and polymer. Used for signaling, crowd control, or military communications in non-combat scenarios.

Other arms excluding those under 9307. Includes spring, air, or gas-powered firearms, as well as truncheons or batons. Materials range from metal to polymer or rubber. Used for non-lethal crowd control, law enforcement, or sporting purposes. May include airsoft guns or gas pistols for training or recreational use.

Ammunition accessories specifically for revolvers or pistols. Includes magazines, clips, or loading mechanisms. Materials are typically metal or polymer. Designed for compatibility with firearms under 9302. Used for reloading, training, or spare parts replacement.

Ammunition accessories for shotguns or rifles under 9303. Includes shells, cartridges, or loading devices. Materials vary but include metal and plastic. Designed for compatibility with firearms under 9303. Used for reloading, target practice, or spare parts.

Accessories for military weapons under 9301. Includes sights, mounts, or specialized attachments. Materials are typically metal or polymer. Designed for compatibility with weapons under 9301. Used for tactical enhancement, maintenance, or specialized combat operations.

Other accessories for firearms not specified elsewhere. Includes holsters, cleaning kits, or non-standard attachments. Materials vary but include leather, metal, or polymer. Used for storage, maintenance, or specialized purposes not covered under other headings.

Cartridges for firearms. These are complete ammunition units including primer, powder, and projectile. Materials include brass, steel, and plastic. Designed for compatibility with various firearms. Used for target shooting, hunting, or military applications.

Other ammunition accessories for air weapons. Includes magazines, clips, or loading mechanisms. Materials typically include metal or polymer. Designed for compatibility with air or gas-powered firearms. Used for reloading, training, or spare parts replacement.

Cartridges specifically for revolvers, pistols, or sub-machine guns. Includes standard or specialized ammunition. Materials include brass, steel, and plastic. Designed for compatibility with firearms under 9301 or 9302. Used for personal defense, law enforcement, or military applications.

Cartridges for military weapons. Includes standard-issue or specialized ammunition. Materials include brass, steel, and polymer. Designed for compatibility with military firearms. Used for combat, training, or reserve stock.

Other cartridges not specified elsewhere. Includes experimental, replica, or non-standard ammunition. Materials vary but include metal and polymer. Used for training, collection, or specialized applications not covered under other headings.

Military-grade bombs and mines. These are explosive devices designed for tactical or strategic deployment. Materials include metal, plastic, or composite. Used for breaching, area denial, or anti-personnel purposes. Typically require detonation mechanisms or remote triggers.

Other bombs and mines not specified elsewhere. Includes experimental, non-military, or civilian-grade explosive devices. Materials vary but include metal, plastic, or composite. Used for training, research, or non-combat applications. May include pyrotechnic or signaling devices.

Swords, cutlasses, bayonets, lances, and similar melee weapons. Includes blades, hilts, and scabbards. Materials include steel, brass, or polymer. Used for ceremonial, historical, or military purposes. May include replicas or antique models for display or training.

Seats designed specifically for aircraft use, typically constructed for passenger or crew comfort during flight. These are fixed or removable units, often with reinforced frames and safety-compliant materials. May include armrests, backrests, and adjustable mechanisms for ergonomic support during air travel.

Miscellaneous grooming or personal care items not otherwise specified, including hairbrushes, grooming kits, or accessories not covered under other HS headings in Chapter 96.
Toilet sprays and similar hygiene products designed for use in restrooms, typically containing cleaning or deodorizing agents, used for personal hygiene or sanitation purposes.

Powder puffs and pads used for applying cosmetics or toiletries, typically made of soft fabric or sponge material, designed for gentle application of powders or creams.
Complete vacuum flasks and other vacuum-insulated containers, including thermoses and insulated bottles, designed to maintain temperature, along with parts excluding glass inner liners.
Mannequin figures and mechanical automata used for display purposes, including tailors’ dummies, shop window displays, or animated figures designed to attract customers or showcase products.
Hygiene pads and liners made of wadding of textile materials, designed for absorbency and comfort, typically used in sanitary products or baby care items.
Sanitary towels (pads), tampons, and similar hygiene products designed for menstrual or incontinence care, typically disposable and absorbent.

Napkins and napkin liners specifically designed for babies, including disposable or reusable absorbent pads for infant hygiene or feeding.

Lenses, mirrors, or optical components made of materials other than plastic or aluminum, used in various optical or electronic devices, including cameras, projectors, or other apparatus.

Paintings, drawings, and pastels, including works on canvas or paper, executed in oil, watercolor, pencil, charcoal, or similar media, intended for artistic display or collection, not for industrial or commercial use.

Paintings, drawings, and pastels, including works on canvas or paper, executed in oil, watercolor, pencil, charcoal, or similar media, intended for artistic display or collection, not for industrial or commercial use.
Mosaics and related collage artworks, composed of small pieces of glass, stone, ceramic, or metal arranged to form decorative or artistic images, typically mounted on walls or floors for display.

Other artworks not classified elsewhere, including mixed media, assemblage, or experimental forms, typically created for artistic or decorative purposes and not for functional or industrial applications.
Stone carvings and sculptures of historical or artistic significance, including monumental or commemorative works, dating back more than 100 years, typically made from marble, granite, or limestone.

Other stone carvings and sculptures, including decorative or utilitarian forms, not exceeding 100 years of age, typically made from marble, granite, or limestone, intended for display or architectural use.

Wooden sculptures and carvings of historical or artistic significance, including monumental or commemorative works, dating back more than 100 years, typically crafted from oak, walnut, or other hardwoods.

Other wooden sculptures and carvings, including decorative or utilitarian forms, not exceeding 100 years of age, typically crafted from oak, walnut, or other hardwoods, intended for display or architectural use.

Postage stamps, revenue stamps, first-day covers, postal stationery, and similar items, including those with or without cancellation marks, not classified under Chapter 4907, for philatelic or historical collection purposes.

Collections and collector’s items of archaeological, ethnographic, or historical interest, including artifacts, tools, textiles, or documents, preserved for educational or cultural display purposes, regardless of origin or material.
Human anatomical specimens or parts thereof, including bones, skulls, or preserved tissues, typically collected for scientific, medical, or educational purposes, not for commercial or artistic use.

Specimens or parts of extinct or endangered species, including bones, shells, or preserved biological materials, collected for scientific, educational, or conservation purposes, not for commercial or artistic use.

Other biological or anatomical specimens, including preserved animal or plant parts, not classified elsewhere, typically collected for scientific or educational purposes, not for commercial or artistic use.

Collections and collector’s items of archaeological, ethnographic, or historical interest, including artifacts, tools, textiles, or documents, dating back more than 100 years, preserved for educational or cultural display purposes.

Other collections and collector’s items, including artifacts, tools, textiles, or documents, not exceeding 100 years of age, preserved for educational or cultural display purposes, regardless of origin or material.

Antiquities, including sculptures, inscriptions, or architectural fragments, dating back more than 250 years, typically of historical or cultural significance, made from stone, metal, or other durable materials.

Goods temporarily imported for repair or maintenance, including machinery or equipment, and intended for re-export after servicing or restoration.
Goods admitted for temporary importation under tax-free conditions, typically for use in specific operations or exhibitions, without immediate duty liability.
Goods temporarily imported for re-export, including oil and related products, stored in bonded warehouses or facilities under customs supervision.
Goods admitted under free circulation or general importation, typically for domestic use without specific restrictions or customs duties.
Goods admitted for temporary importation with no specific restrictions, often used for exhibitions, testing, or experimental purposes.
Goods in transit, temporarily admitted for movement through customs territory without duty payment, intended for onward shipment to another country.
